meanings.definition: Tending to cause harm; damaging; injurious.
meanings.example: The regulator found that the practice was detrimental to consumers and ordered it to cease within thirty days.

meanings.definition: A state of disorganisation or untidiness; a confused state.
meanings.example: The policy launch descended into disarray when it emerged that two departments had published contradictory guidance on the same day.

meanings.definition: To disturb the composure of someone; to unsettle or fluster.
meanings.example: The unexpected question from the panel chair disconcerted the candidate, who had prepared thoroughly but not for this particular line of enquiry.

meanings.definition: Showing a lack of respect combined with a feeling of superiority; contemptuous.
meanings.example: Her disdainful expression during the presentation was noticed by everyone in the room and made it clear she considered the proposal inadequate.

meanings.definition: A great difference between two things; inequality or incongruity.
meanings.example: The audit revealed a significant disparity between the figures reported internally and those presented to external stakeholders.

meanings.definition: To be indecisive; to act nervously and in a confused way; to waste time through indecision.
meanings.example: While leadership dithered over whether to issue a statement, the story developed a life of its own in the absence of an official response.

meanings.definition: The action of evading something; avoidance, especially of duty or question; dishonest escape.
meanings.example: The evasion of direct questions during the hearing was noted by journalists and became a story in its own right.

meanings.definition: A feeling of intense irritation or frustration.
meanings.example: The researcher could barely conceal her exasperation when the data she had spent six months collecting proved incompatible with the team's analysis tools.

meanings.definition: Serving as a desirable model; representing the best possible standard; serving as a warning or deterrent.
meanings.example: The committee cited the project as exemplary, pointing to it as a model that other teams across the department should be encouraged to replicate.

meanings.definition: Patient and self-controlled; showing restraint under provocation; tolerant.
meanings.example: The mediator's forbearing approach to the most antagonistic of the participants eventually earned a degree of trust that the direct intervention had failed to achieve.

meanings.definition: Lacking ease or grace; unsophisticated and socially awkward.
meanings.example: His gauche manner at the reception, enthusiastically name-dropping people he had met briefly, made experienced diplomats visibly uncomfortable.

meanings.definition: Characteristic of or relating to a class or group; not specific or particular; sold without a brand name.
meanings.example: The response was so generic that it could have been copied from a template and bore no relationship to the specific concerns raised.

meanings.definition: Truly what something is said to be; authentic; free from pretence; sincere.
meanings.example: Whether the apology was genuine or a calculated PR exercise was a matter of debate, but its timing was politically convenient.

meanings.definition: Very severe or serious; causing or involving great pain or suffering.
meanings.example: The tribunal described the failures as grievous, emphasising that the harm caused was entirely foreseeable and could have been prevented.

meanings.definition: Easily persuaded to believe something; lacking critical judgement; credulous.
meanings.example: The fraud succeeded largely because investors wanted to believe the returns were real and were not sufficiently gullible to question the mechanisms claimed.

meanings.definition: Arrogantly superior and disdainful; behaving as if others are not worthy of one's regard.
meanings.example: Her haughty dismissal of the question insulted a delegate who had raised it in genuine good faith, creating unnecessary tension.

meanings.definition: Energetically wilful and determined to have one's own way; obstinately self-willed.
meanings.example: His headstrong refusal to consult with colleagues before making announcements repeatedly undermined the policy process.

meanings.definition: Not feeling or showing emotion; calm and serene; not moved.
meanings.example: His impassive expression throughout the proceedings gave nothing away to those watching for signs of how the verdict might go.

meanings.definition: Without intention; accidentally; without realising what one is doing.
meanings.example: The press officer inadvertently disclosed the information by including the wrong version of the document in the briefing pack.

meanings.definition: Lacking sense or meaning; silly and pointless; vacuous.
meanings.example: The inane small talk that preceded the meeting consumed twenty minutes that could have been spent on the substantive agenda items.

meanings.definition: Causing trouble, difficulties, or discomfort; not fitting in with one's needs or plans.
meanings.example: The timing of the report was inconvenient for the administration, arriving as it did three weeks before the election.

meanings.definition: Feeling or showing anger or annoyance at what is perceived as unfair treatment.
meanings.example: The committee members were indignant at the implication that they had been aware of the irregularities and had failed to act.

meanings.definition: Impossible to subdue or defeat; relentlessly determined.
meanings.example: Her indomitable spirit through years of setbacks became central to the mythology surrounding the campaign's eventual success.

meanings.definition: Unable to be explained or accounted for; incomprehensible.
meanings.example: The decision to proceed without the legal advice seemed inexplicable to observers who later reviewed the file.

meanings.definition: Impossible to disentangle from or separate; too complex to resolve.
meanings.example: The political and economic dimensions of the crisis were inextricable: any solution to one would require changes in the other.

meanings.definition: To make someone extremely angry and impatient.
meanings.example: The lack of a direct answer to a direct question infuriated questioners who had been waiting three months for a substantive response.

meanings.definition: To write or carve words or symbols on a surface; to dedicate a book or drawing to someone with a handwritten message.
meanings.example: The dedication inscribed in the front of the volume suggested a closer relationship between the two scholars than the official record had disclosed.

meanings.definition: Having or showing accurate and deep understanding; perceptive.
meanings.example: The most insightful observation in the review came from the junior member, who asked why the market-entry assumptions had not been tested before capital was committed.

meanings.definition: Impossible to solve; incapable of being dissolved.
meanings.example: The fundamental conflict of interest was insoluble by any procedural adjustment short of one party withdrawing from the process entirely.

meanings.definition: Too extreme to bear; intolerable; excessively arrogant or conceited.
meanings.example: His insufferable habit of interrupting colleagues mid-sentence was well known but had gone unaddressed because of his position in the hierarchy.

meanings.definition: To frighten or overawe someone, especially so as to make them do what one wants.
meanings.example: The email, later adduced as evidence, was found to have been designed to intimidate the witness before her appearance before the tribunal.

meanings.definition: Belonging naturally; essential; inherent in the nature of something.
meanings.example: The intrinsic value of the asset, independent of market sentiment, was estimated to be significantly lower than its quoted price.

meanings.definition: To speak or write about something with great hostility.
meanings.example: The editorial inveighed against the decision, using language that struck more measured commentators as excessive even by the standards of the debate.

meanings.definition: Requiring considerable effort and time; tedious and painstaking; not natural or fluent.
meanings.example: The laborious process of establishing identity through paper records could have been shortened considerably by implementing the electronic verification system.

meanings.definition: Displaying or having a disinclination for physical exertion or effort; slow and relaxed.
meanings.example: The languid pace of the negotiations belied the urgency of the situation and frustrated those outside the room who were watching events unfold.

meanings.definition: To praise highly; to acclaim.
meanings.example: The report lauded the team's responsiveness but questioned whether the underlying problem had been systematically addressed.

meanings.definition: A lack of energy and enthusiasm; tiredness; a state of sluggishness.
meanings.example: The lethargy that had settled over the organisation after the failed merger attempt took more than a year to lift.

meanings.definition: Of imposing height; elevated in character or status; noble and impressive; haughty.
meanings.example: The lofty ambitions stated in the founding documents had given way, over time, to a pragmatic focus on institutional survival.

meanings.definition: About to happen and seeming threatening; emerging into view with an indistinct or threatening form.
meanings.example: The looming deadline concentrated minds in a way that months of discussion had failed to achieve.

meanings.definition: A scheming or crafty action or artful design intended to accomplish some usually evil end; a plot.
meanings.example: The tribunal found evidence of machination behind the scenes, with communications revealing a coordinated effort to obstruct the inquiry.

meanings.definition: To become apparent through the appearance of symptoms; to display or show a quality by one's acts or appearance.
meanings.example: The stress of the role manifested in physical symptoms that were not identified or treated until the individual had been signed off sick.

meanings.definition: A short statement expressing a general truth or rule of conduct; an aphorism or proverb.
meanings.example: The team operated according to the maxim that a problem identified early is half solved, and invested heavily in monitoring.

meanings.definition: Lacking in quantity or quality; sparse and unsatisfying.
meanings.example: The meagre evidence base underlying the proposal was identified in the impact assessment but did not prevent the policy from proceeding.

meanings.definition: A wrong or inaccurate name or designation; a wrong or inaccurate use of a name or term.
meanings.example: Calling the policy a 'reform' was a misnomer, as it reversed almost every significant change that the previous administration had introduced.

meanings.definition: So small or unimportant as to be not worth considering; insignificant.
meanings.example: The difference in cost between the two options was negligible, and the decision was therefore taken on grounds of quality and delivery.

meanings.definition: The practice among those with power or influence of favouring relatives or friends, especially by giving them jobs.
meanings.example: The appointment of the director's son-in-law to a senior role without open competition led to allegations of nepotism that were never fully resolved.

meanings.definition: Extremely unpleasant; repulsive; causing strong dislike.
meanings.example: The report described the conditions in the facility as odious and called for immediate remedial action.

meanings.definition: Comprehensive; forming an arch over something; dominant in authority or importance.
meanings.example: The overarching objective of the programme was to improve long-term outcomes, with specific targets being designed to serve that larger aim.

meanings.definition: Done openly; not secret; easily detected.
meanings.example: The overt hostility between the two departments made any attempt at collaboration difficult, as both sides arrived at joint meetings expecting failure.

meanings.definition: A solution or remedy for all difficulties or diseases; a cure-all.
meanings.example: Decentralisation was proposed as a panacea, but the evidence from comparable jurisdictions suggested its effects were mixed and context-dependent.

meanings.definition: Extreme poverty; lack of resources.
meanings.example: The charity documented cases of elderly people in genuine penury despite being theoretically entitled to benefits they had not applied for.

meanings.definition: Childishly sulky or bad-tempered behaviour; peevishness.
meanings.example: The petulance with which he responded to criticism made it harder for supporters to defend him in the coverage that followed.

meanings.definition: To obtain something, especially with care or effort; to bring about an outcome; to acquire through purchase.
meanings.example: The department was tasked with procuring the necessary equipment within budget and to specification, a challenge made harder by supply-chain disruptions.

meanings.definition: Serving to prohibit or forbid something; (of a price or cost) excessively high.
meanings.example: The cost of compliance was described as prohibitive for small businesses, effectively excluding them from the regulated market.

meanings.definition: To succeed in material terms; to be financially successful; to flourish.
meanings.example: The region had prospered during the commodity boom and was now struggling to diversify as prices fell.

meanings.definition: To express sharp disapproval or criticism of someone because of their behaviour or actions.
meanings.example: The regulator rebuked the bank in terms that left no doubt about the seriousness of the finding, even though it stopped short of imposing a fine.

meanings.definition: To say that one no longer holds a previously stated opinion or belief; to withdraw formally.
meanings.example: Under intense cross-examination, the witness recanted the key part of his earlier statement, fatally undermining the prosecution case.

meanings.definition: Avoiding the company of other people; living in seclusion; solitary.
meanings.example: In his final years, the former chairman became increasingly reclusive, refusing all media requests and conducting business through intermediaries.

meanings.definition: Extremely distasteful; unacceptable; contradictory or inconsistent with something.
meanings.example: The committee found the proposed terms repugnant to basic principles of natural justice and recommended they be removed.

meanings.definition: Admirably purposeful, determined, and unwavering in one's objectives.
meanings.example: Despite three years of setbacks, the team remained resolute in its commitment to the original design principles.

meanings.definition: To feel deep respect and admiration for someone or something; to venerate.
meanings.example: He was revered within the institution as the person who had almost single-handedly built the research programme from nothing.

meanings.definition: An action intended to deceive someone; a trick or stratagem.
meanings.example: The apparent offer to negotiate was later revealed as a ruse designed to buy time while the other side secured external financing.

meanings.definition: A person inclined to question or doubt accepted opinions; one who doubts the validity of a claim.
meanings.example: Even committed sceptics found it difficult to dismiss the volume of evidence assembled by the inquiry, though they continued to dispute some of the methodology.

meanings.definition: A split or division between strongly opposed sections or parties, caused by differences in opinion or belief.
meanings.example: The disagreement over membership conditions caused a schism in the organisation that took a decade to repair.

meanings.definition: Exaggerated or indulged tenderness, sadness, or nostalgia; excessive emotional feeling.
meanings.example: Critics accused the film of retreating into sentimentality when it needed to confront its subject with greater honesty.

meanings.definition: The state of being subject to another person as owner; subjugation or bondage.
meanings.example: The contract conditions were so restrictive that several commentators compared them to a form of economic servitude.

meanings.definition: A custom, phrase, or use of language regarded as distinguishing a particular group; an outdated or discredited doctrine.
meanings.example: The requirement to use the specific terminology had become something of a shibboleth, signalling membership of the intellectual tradition rather than contributing clarity.

meanings.definition: The use of clever but false arguments, especially with the intention of deceiving; specious reasoning.
meanings.example: The committee saw through the sophistry of the submission, identifying the logical flaw underlying what appeared superficially to be a rigorous case.

meanings.definition: To reject with disdain or contempt; to refuse to accept or use.
meanings.example: She spurned the offer of a settlement, believing that accepting it would imply complicity in conduct she considered unlawful.

meanings.definition: To waste money, time, or other resources in a reckless way.
meanings.example: The inquiry found that the organisation had squandered the majority of its reserves on a vanity project that delivered no measurable benefit.

meanings.definition: To cause something to appear foolish or absurd; to cause to lose enthusiasm and initiative.
meanings.example: Years of bureaucratic obstruction had stultified what was once one of the most innovative research groups in the country.

meanings.definition: To suppose something without having full evidence; to conjecture.
meanings.example: The investigator could only surmise that the documents had been destroyed deliberately, as there was no record of when or by whom.

meanings.definition: Repeating the same point in different words; saying the same thing twice; redundant.
meanings.example: The argument was tautological, defining the conclusion in terms of the premise and adding nothing to what was already asserted.

meanings.definition: A strip of wood, metal, or stone forming the bottom of a doorway; the magnitude or intensity that must be exceeded for a reaction to occur.
meanings.example: The evidence passed the threshold required by law for the case to proceed to trial, though it remained far short of conclusive.

meanings.definition: Full of twists and turns; excessively complex and indirect; convoluted.
meanings.example: The tortuous regulatory process required approval from seven separate bodies before work could begin, a requirement that had deterred several potential investors.

meanings.definition: Easy to deal with; docile; (of a problem) easy to handle.
meanings.example: The data problem proved more tractable than feared once the team agreed on a common definition of the variable in question.

meanings.definition: Not right or reasonable; shockingly unfair or unjust; greatly exceeding normal bounds.
meanings.example: The tribunal described the terms of the agreement as unconscionable and set them aside as contrary to public policy.

meanings.definition: Not intimidated or discouraged by difficulty, danger, or disappointment.
meanings.example: Undaunted by the initial rejection, the team revised the application and resubmitted it with additional evidence that addressed the committee's concerns.

meanings.definition: To describe something as being smaller, worse, or less important than it really is.
meanings.example: The impact assessment significantly understated the distributional effects, a flaw identified by the external reviewer but not corrected before publication.

meanings.definition: To an unwarranted degree; excessively; in a way that is not justified by the circumstances.
meanings.example: The sentence was found to be unduly lenient on appeal and was increased to reflect the gravity and premeditation of the offending.

meanings.definition: Not showing fear or hesitation in the face of danger or difficulty; steadfast.
meanings.example: Her unflinching examination of the most uncomfortable findings in the data set raised the quality of the whole report.

meanings.definition: Absolute and total; not reduced in severity, intensity, or severity by any qualification.
meanings.example: The project was described by the review panel as an unmitigated failure, with no redeeming outcomes that could justify the expenditure.

meanings.definition: Difficult to put up with; not acceptable; (of food) not pleasant to taste.
meanings.example: The findings were unpalatable to the leadership, who delayed publication in the hope that subsequent developments would soften their political impact.

meanings.definition: Not justified or authorised; not warranted; unnecessary or disproportionate.
meanings.example: The delay was described as unwarranted, given that all the necessary information had been available for months.

meanings.definition: Not giving way to pressure; firm; refusing to change one's position or course of action.
meanings.example: The committee's unyielding stance on the clause prevented an agreement from being reached before the deadline.

meanings.definition: To check or prove the validity or accuracy of something; to make or declare legally valid; to confirm.
meanings.example: The independent review was commissioned to validate the internal findings before they were presented to the board.

meanings.definition: Intentional; deliberate; having or showing a stubborn and determined intention to do as one wants.
meanings.example: The tribunal found the breach to be willful, noting that the respondent had been warned on three separate occasions and had taken no remedial action.

meanings.definition: A thing that causes extreme disgust or hatred; something morally reprehensible.
meanings.example: The committee described the conditions in the facility as an abomination that had no place in a society governed by the rule of law.

meanings.definition: Obsequious flattery or excessive admiration.
meanings.example: The adulation heaped on the new chief executive obscured the very real challenges she would face once the honeymoon period ended.

meanings.definition: To campaign energetically for a cause; to make someone troubled or nervous; to stir up physically.
meanings.example: Labour activists had agitated for the reform for over a decade before it finally reached the legislative agenda.

meanings.definition: To cause someone to feel isolated or estranged; to make hostile or unsympathetic; to transfer ownership.
meanings.example: The decision to proceed without consultation alienated the very stakeholders whose support would be needed for successful implementation.

meanings.definition: A noisy argument or disagreement, especially in public.
meanings.example: The altercation between the two committee members during the evidence session was later cited as an example of the breakdown in working relations.

meanings.definition: Self-confidence or assurance, especially in a demanding situation; composure.
meanings.example: She handled the hostile questioning with aplomb, neither conceding ground nor appearing rattled by the persistent interruptions.

meanings.definition: To arrest someone for a crime; to understand or perceive; to dread.
meanings.example: The fugitive was eventually apprehended at the border, two weeks after the international arrest warrant had been issued.

meanings.definition: Appropriate or suitable in the circumstances; quick to learn or understand.
meanings.example: The metaphor was apt, capturing in a single image the complexity of the relationship between the two institutions.

meanings.definition: Without deceit or pretension; natural and sincere; without guile; artistically inept.
meanings.example: Her artless account of events, delivered without any apparent concern for how it would reflect on her, proved more compelling than the carefully prepared testimony of others.

meanings.definition: Showing a willingness to take surprisingly bold risks; showing an impudent lack of respect.
meanings.example: The audacious strategy of confronting the regulator directly rather than appealing through normal channels paid off when the ruling was unexpectedly reversed.

meanings.definition: The playful and friendly exchange of teasing remarks.
meanings.example: The banter between the two ministers had become so familiar a feature of the dispatch box exchanges that journalists had begun to mock it as choreographed.

meanings.definition: To make a gesture to encourage someone to come; to seem to be attractively inviting.
meanings.example: Increasingly lucrative opportunities outside the public sector beckoned the most talented officials, accelerating the loss of institutional knowledge.

meanings.definition: To confuse or perplex someone; to make someone unable to think clearly, especially through alcohol.
meanings.example: The contradictory guidance issued by three different departments befuddled practitioners trying to advise their clients in good faith.

meanings.definition: To scold or criticise someone angrily.
meanings.example: She berated the press officer for releasing the statement without checking it against the revised figures that had arrived that morning.

meanings.definition: To cause someone to become perplexed and confused; to puzzle completely.
meanings.example: The absence of any coherent framework bewildered observers trying to make sense of what the policy was actually intended to achieve.

meanings.definition: Obstinate or unreasonable attachment to a belief, opinion, or faction; intolerance towards those who differ.
meanings.example: The inquiry found evidence of bigotry in recruitment decisions, with protected characteristics clearly influencing the selection outcomes.

meanings.definition: Affected by blight; ruined; spoiled; affected by a disease or other damaging condition.
meanings.example: The blighted neighbourhood had been identified as a priority for regeneration funding, but three successive plans had failed to gain traction.

meanings.definition: A thing that is helpful or beneficial; a timely blessing or benefit; a favour sought.
meanings.example: The unexpectedly favourable exchange rate proved a boon to exporters who had been squeezed by rising input costs.

meanings.definition: Large in quantity; abundant; giving generously.
meanings.example: The harvest of empirical data generated by the new monitoring systems provided a bountiful resource for researchers across the field.

meanings.definition: To examine material and suppress or remove anything deemed objectionable; to act as a censor.
meanings.example: The version submitted to the regulator had been censored to remove the most damaging internal assessments that appeared in the original document.

meanings.definition: Making a loud and confused noise; urgently expressing demand or discontent.
meanings.example: A clamorous crowd had gathered outside the building before the announcement was made, and the noise could be heard inside the hearing chamber.

meanings.definition: A covered walk in a convent, monastery, college, or cathedral; to keep away from the outside world; to seclude.
meanings.example: The researcher spent two years cloistered in the archives, emerging only to present preliminary findings to a small group of specialists.

meanings.definition: To officially take possession or control of something for military or official use; to seize arbitrarily.
meanings.example: Emergency powers were invoked to commandeer private vehicles, a step regarded as necessary given the scale of the evacuation required.

meanings.definition: The patronising attitude of treating others as inferior; an act of showing superiority.
meanings.example: The condescension evident in the response — explaining basic facts to experts in the field — was noted and resented by the audience.

meanings.definition: To take or seize someone's property with authority; to take possession of as a penalty.
meanings.example: The regulator confiscated the files as part of the investigation into the alleged manipulation of financial benchmarks.

meanings.definition: A person who conforms to accepted behaviour or established practices; tending to conform; conventional.
meanings.example: The culture had become so conformist that genuinely original ideas were met with scepticism rather than curiosity, limiting the organisation's ability to adapt.

meanings.definition: To make something narrower or tighter; to restrict or inhibit.
meanings.example: The new compliance requirements would constrict the flow of capital in ways that could reduce the efficiency of markets the regulation was intended to support.

meanings.definition: Warm and friendly; (of a drink) sweet fruit flavoured.
meanings.example: Despite the fundamental disagreement on the underlying issue, the meeting was conducted in a cordial atmosphere that made further dialogue possible.

meanings.definition: To revoke or cancel a command or order; to order the recall of.
meanings.example: The field commander countermanded the earlier order when it became clear that the information on which it had been based was incorrect.

meanings.definition: Contemptuous ridicule or mockery.
meanings.example: The proposal was greeted with derision by opponents who described it as naive and entirely disconnected from the realities of implementation.

meanings.definition: In low spirits from loss of hope or courage; dejected.
meanings.example: Staff became despondent when the promised restructuring was delayed for the third time in eighteen months, with no clear explanation offered for the repeated postponements.

meanings.definition: To disconnect or separate something; to send a group away from the main body; to become objective.
meanings.example: The committee was advised to detach itself from the political dimensions of the question and focus solely on the technical evidence.

meanings.definition: To free from enchantment; to cause to be disillusioned; to deprive of belief in an ideal.
meanings.example: A series of broken promises had disenchanted the most loyal supporters of the initiative, who had invested considerable energy in making it work.

meanings.definition: To cause someone to realise that a belief or ideal is false; to disappoint someone who had a good opinion of something.
meanings.example: The revelations thoroughly disillusioned those who had believed the organisation's public commitment to transparency was genuine.

meanings.definition: To regard or represent as being of little worth; to belittle or speak critically of.
meanings.example: He was careful never publicly to disparage his predecessors, though privately his assessment of their record was harsh.

meanings.definition: Having normal physical functions suspended or slowed down for a period of time; temporarily inactive.
meanings.example: The volcano had been dormant for four centuries, and the research team aimed to determine whether that quiescence was likely to continue.

meanings.definition: Not operating normally or properly; deviating from the norms of social behaviour; impaired.
meanings.example: The audit identified the finance team as dysfunctional, with unclear responsibilities, duplicated effort, and a persistent failure to meet reporting deadlines.

meanings.definition: To make something more attractive by adding decorative details; to add fictitious details to a story.
meanings.example: The witness had clearly embellished his account, adding details that contradicted the documentary evidence and had not appeared in his original statement.

meanings.definition: Famous and respected within a particular sphere; above others in stature or distinction.
meanings.example: An eminent economist who had previously been sceptical of the policy was among those who eventually endorsed the revised proposal.

meanings.definition: To gradually intrude on another's territory, rights, or domain; to advance beyond due limits.
meanings.example: The new regulatory framework was seen as encroaching on decisions that had traditionally been left to market participants.

meanings.definition: To declare public approval or support of; to recommend a product or policy; to sign the back of a cheque.
meanings.example: The independent panel endorsed the findings, noting that it had examined the underlying data and found no methodological flaws.

meanings.definition: A messenger or representative, especially one on a diplomatic mission; a representative sent by a government or organisation.
meanings.example: The special envoy arrived in the capital to begin the mediation process that both parties had agreed to as a precondition for further negotiations.

meanings.definition: Not even or regular in pattern or movement; unpredictable; inconsistent in quality.
meanings.example: The erratic performance of the model across different test conditions led the team to question whether the underlying assumptions were sound.

meanings.definition: To become more serious, intense, or extensive; to increase rapidly; to raise a matter to a higher level.
meanings.example: The dispute escalated from a technical disagreement into a full governance crisis when one party involved the board without prior notice.

meanings.definition: Bringing strong memories, images, or feelings to mind; having the power to evoke.
meanings.example: The evidence was presented in language deliberately evocative of the harm caused, designed to leave an impression beyond what the data alone would convey.

meanings.definition: Possible to do easily or conveniently; probable; likely.
meanings.example: The technical assessment concluded that the proposal was feasible within the budget envelope, though it noted a number of implementation risks that would require management.

meanings.definition: A thing that is a complete failure, especially in a ludicrous or humiliating way.
meanings.example: The product launch was widely described as a fiasco after the website crashed within minutes and customer service lines were overwhelmed.

meanings.definition: Not real or true; invented; false; relating to fiction.
meanings.example: The investigators found that many of the referenced transactions were fictitious, created to support a fraudulent picture of financial activity.

meanings.definition: An outward form or appearance, especially when assumed as a disguise; pretext.
meanings.example: The policy change was presented under the guise of simplification, but the substantive effect was a significant restriction on the powers of the relevant authority.

meanings.definition: Severe suffering or privation; a condition that is difficult to endure.
meanings.example: The impact assessment documented cases of genuine hardship caused by the sudden withdrawal of the entitlement, without adequate notice or transitional support.

meanings.definition: Done with excessive speed or urgency; hurried; rash or inconsiderate.
meanings.example: The decision was widely criticised as hasty, reached without adequate consultation and on the basis of an incomplete understanding of the operational context.

meanings.definition: Avoiding work; lazy; not working or active; without purpose or effect.
meanings.example: Resources were sitting idle for weeks while the procurement process took longer than expected, adding unnecessary cost to the programme.

meanings.definition: To have an effect or impact, especially a negative one; to encroach or infringe on.
meanings.example: The new policy was found to impinge on rights that were protected by the existing legislative framework and had to be substantially revised.

meanings.definition: To show someone to be involved in a crime or wrongdoing; to convey a particular meaning by suggestion.
meanings.example: The documents implicated senior management in the decision, which had previously been attributed to a single mid-level official acting without authorisation.

meanings.definition: Grand and impressive; having a strong impact in appearance; demanding attention.
meanings.example: The imposing architecture of the building was intended to convey permanence and institutional authority, in contrast to the modest offices the organisation had previously occupied.

meanings.definition: To make poor; to weaken or deprive of strength, vitality, or other good qualities.
meanings.example: Decades of under-investment had impoverished the soil and reduced crop yields to a fraction of what the land had previously sustained.

meanings.definition: So opposed in character as to be incapable of existing together; inconsistent with.
meanings.example: The two legal obligations were incompatible, and the organisation sought clarification from the regulator on how to prioritise them.

meanings.definition: Not corresponding or in harmony with; out of place; incompatible.
meanings.example: The informal tone of the communication was incongruent with the gravity of the decision it was intended to convey.

meanings.definition: The fact or state of being inconsistent; a difference or discrepancy between things that should be the same.
meanings.example: The inconsistency between the figures in the report and those in the underlying data tables was identified as a potential indicator of a more serious error.

meanings.definition: Poor and needy; having insufficient means to live on.
meanings.example: The legal aid scheme was specifically designed to ensure that indigent defendants had access to representation of the same quality as those who could pay privately.

meanings.definition: Curious; eager to acquire knowledge or information; intrusive in seeking information.
meanings.example: An inquisitive press had begun to ask questions that the administration was unprepared to answer, suggesting that the briefing strategy needed rethinking.

meanings.definition: Relating to or characteristic of an inquisition; making persistent and intensive enquiry in a searching or aggressive way.
meanings.example: The inquisitorial style of the hearing alienated some witnesses and reduced the quality of evidence the panel received.

meanings.definition: The state of being insufficient; lack of adequate provision; not enough.
meanings.example: The panel identified an insufficiency of evidence in the core chapter and declined to reach the firm conclusion the authors had sought to establish.

meanings.definition: To intervene on behalf of another person; to plead on someone's behalf; to interpose.
meanings.example: Several senior colleagues interceded with the oversight body to request a more measured response to what they characterised as a first and minor infringement.

meanings.definition: A pause or break between two parts of something; an intervening period or space; a short piece of entertainment between acts.
meanings.example: The interlude provided both sides with time to reassess their positions without the pressure of the formal negotiating environment.

meanings.definition: Endless or apparently endless; so long that one wishes it would stop.
meanings.example: The interminable delays in the approval process had consumed resources that could have been directed towards implementation.

meanings.definition: To place between two things; to intervene between parties; to introduce a comment or objection.
meanings.example: The chair interposed a procedural question to interrupt an exchange that was becoming increasingly personal rather than substantive.

meanings.definition: To irritate or annoy someone; to cause discomfort or irritation.
meanings.example: What irked the investigators most was not the error itself but the deliberate attempt to conceal it once it had been identified.

meanings.definition: To throw or drop from a ship or aircraft; to abandon or discard something that is unsuccessful or burdensome.
meanings.example: The board decided to jettison the legacy platform after the third failed attempt to upgrade it, accepting the sunk cost and starting fresh.

meanings.definition: An acquired or natural skill at performing a task; a tendency to do something.
meanings.example: She had a knack for distilling complex evidence into a form that non-specialist committee members could use to reach informed conclusions.

meanings.definition: To criticise harshly and severely; to beat soundly.
meanings.example: The review lambasted the commissioning process as opaque and poorly managed, and called for an independent inquiry into how the contract had been awarded.

meanings.definition: To publicly criticise someone or something by using ridicule, irony, or sarcasm; a satirical attack.
meanings.example: The decision was lampooned in the press, with cartoonists finding obvious targets in the apparent gap between the stated rationale and the likely outcome.

meanings.definition: To lose or lack vitality; to suffer from being forced to remain in an unpleasant situation; to fail to make progress.
meanings.example: The proposal had languished in committee for two years before a change of personnel brought it back to the active agenda.

meanings.definition: Not sufficiently strict, severe, or careful; loose; relaxed.
meanings.example: The investigation found that oversight had been lax over several years, creating conditions in which the misconduct could go undetected.

meanings.definition: To make something legitimate; to justify or sanction the existence or authority of something.
meanings.example: The external endorsement was sought precisely to legitimise the approach, providing cover against later challenge from critics who had been excluded from the process.

meanings.definition: To stay in a place longer than necessary because of reluctance to leave; to remain for a long time.
meanings.example: The uncertainty lingered well after the announcement, as the market waited for details of implementation that were not forthcoming.

meanings.definition: Lacking energy or enthusiasm; languid; feeling too tired or bored to be interested in anything.
meanings.example: The listless performance during the early months of the programme was attributed to poor goal-setting and a lack of visible senior sponsorship.

meanings.definition: So foolish, unreasonable, or out of place as to be amusing; ridiculous.
meanings.example: The claim that the error had gone unnoticed for three years was described as ludicrous by the specialist, given the volume of data indicating the anomaly.

meanings.definition: Only moderately warm; barely warm; showing little enthusiasm or interest.
meanings.example: The response from industry stakeholders was lukewarm, indicating neither active support nor opposition but a general sense that the consultation had arrived too late.

meanings.definition: To pretend to be someone or something one is not; a false show or pretence.
meanings.example: The restructuring was described by critics as a masquerade, preserving the same problematic incentive structures under a new name.

meanings.definition: To interfere in something that is not one's concern; to handle something carelessly.
meanings.example: The concern was that central government was meddling in decisions that were properly the responsibility of local authorities under existing legislation.

meanings.definition: To organise and encourage a group to take action; to prepare troops for war; to make available for use.
meanings.example: The campaign was designed to mobilise supporters who had not previously been engaged and to convert passive sympathy into active participation.

meanings.definition: To make or become less extreme, intense, or severe; to preside over a discussion; (adjective) not extreme.
meanings.example: The panel was asked to moderate its recommendations to increase the likelihood of broad political acceptance, a request that some members found compromising.

meanings.definition: To nullify or make ineffective; to deny the existence of; to make negative.
meanings.example: The subsequent finding negated the positive outcome of the trial by showing that the effect size was too small to be clinically meaningful.

meanings.definition: To make unclear or difficult to understand; to conceal; not discovered or known; not famous.
meanings.example: The complexity of the structure appeared designed to obscure the true ownership of the assets from regulators.

meanings.definition: Relieving pain or alleviating a problem without dealing with the underlying cause; a remedy with only a superficial effect.
meanings.example: The additional funding was a palliative measure that eased the immediate pressure without resolving the structural deficit.

meanings.definition: Relating to or used for the punishment of offenders; used as a punishment; very severe.
meanings.example: The penal code had not been substantially reviewed for over thirty years, and several provisions were found to be incompatible with current human rights standards.

meanings.definition: To impose a penalty on someone for breaking a rule or law; to put in an unfair disadvantage.
meanings.example: The regulation effectively penalised businesses that had made early investments in compliance by extending the same benefits to later adopters.

meanings.definition: Feeling or expressing sorrow for wrongdoing; contrite.
meanings.example: His penitent tone in the public apology was noted, but observers questioned whether it was matched by any substantive change in the practices that had caused the harm.

meanings.definition: To continue in a course of action in spite of difficulty or with little or no prospect of success.
meanings.example: The team persevered with the methodology despite early setbacks, and the eventual results justified the additional time and effort.

meanings.definition: Remarkable or exceptional; perceptible by the senses; relating to phenomena.
meanings.example: The growth achieved in the first year was phenomenal by any historic comparison, though analysts cautioned against projecting the trajectory forward.

meanings.definition: A sign or warning of a future event, especially an unpleasant one; a person or thing of remarkable or startling abilities.
meanings.example: The early data were read as portents of the difficulties to come, though the warnings were not heeded in time to change course.

meanings.definition: Contrary to reason or common sense; utterly absurd or ridiculous.
meanings.example: The suggestion that the records had been accidentally destroyed twice in succession was described as preposterous by every expert who reviewed the evidence.

meanings.definition: To prove more powerful; to be victorious; to persuade; to be widespread or current.
meanings.example: Despite sustained opposition, the original proposal prevailed after senior leadership made clear that the decision was final and not subject to further revision.

meanings.definition: Spending resources freely and recklessly; having or giving in abundance; lavishly wasteful.
meanings.example: The prodigal expenditure on the refurbishment, far exceeding the approved budget, became the subject of a separate audit investigation.

meanings.definition: To spread and promote widely; to reproduce by natural processes; to transmit or pass something on.
meanings.example: The misinformation spread rapidly once it was propagated through networks that prioritised engagement over accuracy.

meanings.definition: To forbid, especially by law; to denounce or condemn something; to outlaw.
meanings.example: The agreement proscribed certain forms of advertising that had previously been permitted and required members to update their marketing codes within ninety days.

meanings.definition: Officially recognised as competent; not fully in agreement; holding the necessary qualifications.
meanings.example: The expert offered only qualified support for the proposal, pointing to implementation risks that the authors had not adequately addressed.

meanings.definition: To cause continuing annoyance or resentment; to be a source of persistent irritation.
meanings.example: What rankled most was not the outcome but the way the process had been handled, without the consultation that precedent and good practice required.

meanings.definition: To attempt to justify behaviour or attitude with logical reasons, even if these are not the true ones; to reorganise a business for greater efficiency; to make rational.
meanings.example: The subsequent statement was widely seen as an attempt to rationalise a decision that had in practice been taken on political rather than evidential grounds.

meanings.definition: To state something again publicly or more firmly; to confirm once more.
meanings.example: The minister reaffirmed the government's commitment to the target, despite growing evidence that the trajectory was incompatible with its achievement.

meanings.definition: To reject someone in an abrupt or ungracious manner; an abrupt rejection.
meanings.example: The initial approach was rebuffed, but the team persisted and a second overture six months later resulted in the opening of formal negotiations.

meanings.definition: To pay someone for services rendered; to compensate for effort, work, or loss.
meanings.example: The independent consultant was remunerated at a rate that reflected the specialist nature of the advice and the urgency with which it was required.

meanings.definition: To revoke or annul a law or congressional act; to abrogate.
meanings.example: The coalition's first act in power was to repeal the legislation passed by its predecessor, restoring the arrangements that had been in place before the previous administration.

meanings.definition: To rebuke formally, especially an official; a formal expression of disapproval.
meanings.example: The regulator reprimanded the firm for the third time in five years, on this occasion issuing a public notice to reflect the seriousness of the breach.

meanings.definition: Relating to or involving the selection of the most suitable people or things; tending to choose carefully; deliberately limited.
meanings.example: The presentation was criticised as selective, having drawn only on evidence that supported the conclusion while omitting material that pointed in the other direction.

meanings.definition: Having a cunning and deceitful nature; working secretly; cleverly surreptitious.
meanings.example: The sly insertion of a clause that substantially changed the effect of the agreement was not noticed until the contract had been executed.

meanings.definition: To ask for or try to obtain something from someone; to accost someone for a purpose; to make a petition.
meanings.example: The committee solicited written submissions from interested parties before determining whether to convene a public hearing.

meanings.definition: Thinly dispersed or scattered; not dense or crowded; meagre.
meanings.example: The evidence was sparse and mostly circumstantial, which made it difficult to reach the standard of proof required for the more serious charge.

meanings.definition: Superficially plausible but actually wrong; misleadingly attractive in appearance.
meanings.example: The committee saw through the specious reasoning, identifying within minutes the logical gap that undermined the apparently well-constructed case.

meanings.definition: Serious and unrelenting, especially in the assertion of authority or exercise of discipline; (of a wind) following behind.
meanings.example: The stern warning issued by the regulator left no doubt that a further breach would result in formal enforcement action rather than another informal caution.

meanings.definition: Loud and harsh in tone; presenting a point of view in an excessively forceful way.
meanings.example: The strident demands of the campaign group made it harder for moderate voices within the coalition to advance a more nuanced version of the same position.

meanings.definition: Without delay and without taking account of normal procedures; immediately and without formality.
meanings.example: The staff member was summarily dismissed after the investigation concluded that the misconduct was of sufficient gravity to preclude the normal warning process.

meanings.definition: Existing or occurring at the surface; not thorough or deep; appearing to be true but not so; concerned only with the obvious.
meanings.example: The review was criticised as superficial, having accepted management representations at face value without testing them against the underlying evidence.

meanings.definition: Extending over a wide area; wide-ranging; affecting many things or people; (of a gesture) wide and flowing.
meanings.example: The sweeping powers conferred by the emergency legislation were intended to be temporary but were still in use five years after the crisis had passed.

meanings.definition: To feel or express sympathy; to agree with or support a view.
meanings.example: He publicly sympathised with the concerns of those affected by the policy change, while maintaining that the decision itself had been correct.

meanings.definition: To bring to an end; to dismiss an employee; to conclude; to end a pregnancy.
meanings.example: The contract was terminated three months early following the contractor's failure to meet the minimum service level thresholds for the second consecutive quarter.

meanings.definition: Done as a symbolic gesture rather than genuinely; a thing representing something else; minimal.
meanings.example: The single appointment of a woman to the board was described as a token gesture that changed nothing about the decision-making culture.

meanings.definition: To allow the existence of something without necessarily approving of it; to endure something with patience.
meanings.example: The organisation had tolerated the practice for years, treating it as a minor irregularity, until a formal complaint forced a proper assessment of its significance.

meanings.definition: Poisonous; relating to or caused by poison; very bad or unpleasant; harmful to relationships.
meanings.example: The toxic culture that had developed over several years was identified as the primary cause of the high turnover among the most experienced staff.

meanings.definition: A loud, confused noise, as produced by a large mass of people; a state of confusion or disorder.
meanings.example: The announcement was made against a backdrop of political tumult that made it difficult to assess how it would be received once the immediate noise had settled.

meanings.definition: Not pretentious or arrogant; modest; not drawing attention to oneself.
meanings.example: Her unassuming manner concealed a sharp analytical mind and a determination to pursue questions to their conclusion regardless of whose interests the answers served.

meanings.definition: Not awake or aware; done without awareness; below the level of conscious perception; not intentional.
meanings.example: The bias was described as unconscious, a product of deeply embedded assumptions about who was suitable for leadership roles rather than any deliberate intention to discriminate.

meanings.definition: Not morally correct; contrary to the principles of good conduct.
meanings.example: Three of the practices identified in the review were found to be unethical, even if they did not amount to a breach of the letter of the rules.

meanings.definition: Impossible to understand; incomprehensible.
meanings.example: The draft was returned to its authors as unintelligible, requiring a complete rewrite before it could be presented to the committee as a working document.

meanings.definition: Not based on or behaving according to what is morally right and fair.
meanings.example: The finding was widely condemned as unjust, particularly by those who had submitted evidence that had been ignored without explanation in the published report.

meanings.definition: To cause to feel anxious or uneasy; to disturb the settled state or arrangement of.
meanings.example: The sudden departure of the chief executive unsettled both staff and investors, who had been given no advance warning and received no explanation.

meanings.definition: To make practical and effective use of; to use.
meanings.example: The team was encouraged to utilise the analytical tools that had been developed for the previous review, adapting them to the specific requirements of the current assignment.

meanings.definition: Causing annoyance, frustration, or worry; troublesome or distressing.
meanings.example: The most vexing aspect of the problem was not its complexity but the absence of any clear mechanism by which a solution could be implemented and enforced.

meanings.definition: To tolerate or accept something; to follow or obey a rule or decision; to stay or remain.
meanings.example: Both parties had agreed to abide by the outcome of the arbitration, a commitment that became significant when the award went against the larger organisation.

meanings.definition: Deviating from what is normal or usual; not typical; unusual.
meanings.example: The auditors flagged a number of abnormal transactions that fell outside the expected pattern and warranted further investigation.

meanings.definition: Causing moral revulsion; extremely bad or unpleasant; inspiring disgust.
meanings.example: The conditions described by the inspector were characterised as abominable, representing a complete failure of the duty of care owed to those in the facility.

meanings.definition: Sudden and unexpected; brief to the point of rudeness; steep.
meanings.example: The abrupt departure of the chief negotiator left both sides uncertain about whether the talks could continue and on what basis.

meanings.definition: To make a feature or quality more noticeable; to emphasise.
meanings.example: The new reporting format accentuated the disparities between regions that had previously been obscured by the aggregated figures.

meanings.definition: Enthusiastic and public praise; to praise enthusiastically and publicly.
meanings.example: The report received widespread acclaim from those who had been pressing for a more rigorous approach to the subject over many years.

meanings.definition: To fit in with the wishes or needs of; to provide lodging for; to have enough room for.
meanings.example: The revised timeline was designed to accommodate the concerns of those who had argued that the original schedule was unrealistic.

meanings.definition: A person who helps another commit a crime; an associate in wrongdoing.
meanings.example: The prosecution alleged that the company had acted as an accomplice to the fraud by providing the administrative infrastructure through which the scheme was operated.

meanings.definition: Required or expected to justify actions or decisions; responsible to someone.
meanings.example: The review found that no individual had been held accountable for the failures identified in the previous assessment, contributing to their recurrence.

meanings.definition: Correct in all details; conforming exactly to truth or fact; not making errors.
meanings.example: The claim was not accurate, and the correction issued three days later attracted far less attention than the original assertion.

meanings.definition: To accept or admit the existence or truth of something; to recognize the authority of; to show gratitude.
meanings.example: The minister acknowledged that errors had been made but declined to identify which decisions had proved incorrect.

meanings.definition: To stick to or remain fixed on a surface; to follow a rule or standard; to remain committed to.
meanings.example: All members were expected to adhere to the code of conduct as a condition of participation, and breaches would be investigated under the formal process.

meanings.definition: Next to or adjoining something else; close to.
meanings.example: The adjacent office block was also under investigation, raising questions about whether the two cases were connected.

meanings.definition: A warning or reprimand; counsel against specific faults; advice.
meanings.example: The admonition issued in the covering letter made clear that the organisation had been given a final opportunity to correct its practices before formal action would be taken.

meanings.definition: Difficulties; misfortune; a condition marked by misfortune.
meanings.example: The organisation had demonstrated its resilience by maintaining its core services through a period of sustained adversity that would have broken less well-managed institutions.

meanings.definition: Feeling resentment at having been unfairly treated; having a grievance.
meanings.example: The aggrieved parties were given an opportunity to present their account of events at a hearing scheduled for the following month.

meanings.definition: Filled with horror or shock; struck with terror or amazement.
meanings.example: Colleagues were aghast when the full extent of the financial irregularities became clear, having had no prior indication that anything was amiss.

meanings.definition: To claim or assert that something is the case, especially without proof; to plead in support.
meanings.example: The complaint alleged systematic discrimination over a period of years, supported by documentary evidence that the respondent contested.

meanings.definition: To suggest or call attention to indirectly; to hint at.
meanings.example: The chair alluded to concerns about the process in her opening remarks but did not address them directly in the formal record.

meanings.definition: Wrong or not in good order; out of the normal order.
meanings.example: Something was clearly amiss from the outset, but it was not until the third week of the project that anyone put the concern formally on record.

meanings.definition: Comparable in certain respects; similar in function but not in origin; corresponding.
meanings.example: The situation was analogous to several well-documented historical cases, which provided useful guidance for how the mediation process might be structured.

meanings.definition: Deviating from what is standard or expected; irregular; inconsistent.
meanings.example: The anomalous results flagged by the monitoring system were initially dismissed as technical artefacts but later proved to indicate a genuine departure from expected behaviour.

meanings.definition: Active hostility or opposition; a mutually opposing relationship between substances or organisms.
meanings.example: The antagonism between the two departments had deep roots in a resource dispute that neither leadership team had ever resolved.

meanings.definition: Able to be trusted as accurate; commanding; proceeding from an authority; official.
meanings.example: The most authoritative account of the events came not from official sources but from a practitioner who had been present throughout.

meanings.definition: Providing supplementary or additional help; supporting; an auxiliary person or thing.
meanings.example: The auxiliary staff recruited for the peak period had not been briefed on the protocol changes that had been introduced in the main workforce the previous month.

meanings.definition: Having an eager desire for something; keenly interested; enthusiastic.
meanings.example: An avid follower of the proceedings, she had read every published document and arrived at the meeting better prepared than most of the formal participants.

meanings.definition: To totally bewilder or perplex someone; to thwart or hinder.
meanings.example: The inconsistency between the stated objectives and the actual design of the programme baffled those tasked with implementing it.

meanings.definition: To attack or assault someone physically or verbally; to explain or argue in excessive detail.
meanings.example: The committee had already heard the point made twice and asked the witness not to belabour it, as time was limited.

meanings.definition: To trouble or threaten persistently; to surround and hem in.
meanings.example: The programme was beset by problems from the outset, with design flaws, funding gaps, and personnel changes all contributing to the early delays.

meanings.definition: The decisive or most important point at issue; a difficult problem.
meanings.example: The crux of the dispute was not the amount claimed but the interpretation of the clause that determined which party bore responsibility for the loss.

meanings.definition: Neatly skilful and quick in one's movements; showing skill and cleverness.
meanings.example: The deft handling of the question allowed the minister to avoid committing to a specific figure without appearing evasive.

meanings.definition: An idiosyncratic belief or impression that is firmly maintained despite being contradicted by reality; a mistaken or misleading opinion.
meanings.example: The board had been operating under the delusion that the regulatory climate would remain unchanged, an assumption that was shattered by the new framework published that autumn.

meanings.definition: Dissatisfaction with one's circumstances; lack of contentment; a person who is dissatisfied.
meanings.example: The discontent among middle management had been growing for years, fuelled by the perception that their concerns were systematically ignored in strategic decisions.

meanings.definition: The quality of behaving or speaking in such a way as to avoid cause offence; the freedom to decide what should be done in a particular situation.
meanings.example: The panel was given wide discretion in determining what evidence to admit, a feature of the process that was both its strength and the source of subsequent challenge.

meanings.definition: To recognise a distinction; to make an unjust distinction in the treatment of people on grounds of race, sex, age, etc; to distinguish between.
meanings.example: The policy discriminated against part-time workers in a way that the tribunal found could not be objectively justified.

meanings.definition: Too large or too small in comparison with something else; not proportional.
meanings.example: The sanction was widely criticised as disproportionate, far exceeding what the breach warranted when measured against any comparable case.

meanings.definition: To interrupt the normal course or unity of; to cause disorder.
meanings.example: The announcement disrupted the carefully managed sequence of events the team had planned to maintain narrative control throughout the launch.

meanings.definition: Seeking to impose a doctrine in all circumstances without regard to practical considerations; dogmatic.
meanings.example: The approach was criticised as doctrinaire, applying an ideological template regardless of the specific conditions that varied considerably between sectors.

meanings.definition: Involving many carefully arranged parts; detailed and complicated; to develop or present in detail.
meanings.example: The elaborate structure of sub-committees and working groups that had been put in place turned out to be an obstacle rather than an enabler of effective decision-making.

meanings.definition: To give someone the authority or power to do something; to make someone stronger and more confident.
meanings.example: The policy was designed to empower front-line staff to make operational decisions without waiting for approval from a management layer that had become a bottleneck.

meanings.definition: To contemplate or conceive of as a possibility; to form a mental picture of.
meanings.example: The original design envisaged a phased rollout, but the pressure to show early results led to a simultaneous launch that overwhelmed capacity.

meanings.definition: To escape or avoid by cleverness or deception; to avoid dealing with something honestly.
meanings.example: The question was evaded in the initial response, leading the interviewer to press again and accept a more specific answer.

meanings.definition: To free someone from an obligation or liability; not subject to a rule or regulation.
meanings.example: Small charities were exempted from the most burdensome reporting requirements on the grounds that the compliance cost would be disproportionate to the regulatory benefit.

meanings.definition: To lose strength or momentum; to speak hesitantly; to stumble or waver.
meanings.example: The programme began to falter when the funding that had been committed for the third phase was diverted to a more politically visible priority.

meanings.definition: Unlikely and unconvincing; implausible.
meanings.example: The explanation offered for the missing documents was described as farfetched by the investigator, who found it inconsistent with the established chronology of events.

meanings.definition: To display ostentatiously; to show off; to ignore a convention openly and unashamedly.
meanings.example: The spending on the refurbishment was described as flaunting resources at a time when core services were being reduced for lack of funds.

meanings.definition: To impose an unwelcome or unnecessary person or thing on someone; to pass off as genuine.
meanings.example: The amendment was seen as an attempt to foist a condition that had been rejected at an earlier stage onto the agreement through a side process.

meanings.definition: The outer or less important part of something; an unconventional or minority group; to border.
meanings.example: What had been a fringe position in academic debate five years earlier had become the orthodox view among practitioners by the time the report was published.

meanings.definition: To reproduce a message in a confused and distorted way; to scramble.
meanings.example: The message had been garbled in transmission, leading the recipient to take actions based on instructions that did not accurately reflect what had been sent.

meanings.definition: Lean and haggard, especially because of suffering, hunger, or age; grim or desolate in appearance.
meanings.example: The gaunt figure of the building, stripped of its cladding and awaiting demolition, had become an emblem of the failed regeneration strategy.

meanings.definition: Dark or poorly lit; causing or feeling depression or despondency; offering little hope.
meanings.example: The economic forecasts presented a gloomy picture, with most scenarios projecting a further contraction before conditions stabilised.

meanings.definition: Given or allowed only reluctantly; reluctant; unwilling.
meanings.example: The concession was made in a grudging manner that left the other party under no illusion that it would be honoured in spirit as well as letter.

meanings.definition: Diverse in character or content; composed of different or dissimilar elements.
meanings.example: The committee drew on the experience of a heterogeneous group of practitioners, ensuring that the findings reflected a genuinely wide range of sectoral contexts.

meanings.definition: A system in which members of an organisation or society are ranked according to relative status or authority; a ranking.
meanings.example: The hierarchy within the organisation was so rigid that junior staff felt unable to pass information upwards without going through multiple intermediary levels.

meanings.definition: To make it difficult for someone or something to do or to progress; to obstruct.
meanings.example: The lack of data-sharing agreements between the relevant agencies hindered the investigation and made it impossible to establish the full picture in the time available.

meanings.definition: Showing or feeling opposition or resistance; unfriendly; of or relating to a military enemy.
meanings.example: The hostile reception from industry stakeholders reflected a deeper disagreement about whether the proposed framework was appropriate for the nature and scale of the businesses it would cover.

meanings.definition: The practice of claiming to have higher standards or more admirable beliefs than one actually possesses.
meanings.example: The hypocrisy at the heart of the position was exposed when documents emerged showing that the official line was contradicted by private assessments shared with advisers.

meanings.definition: Not showing proper respect; irrelevant or inappropriate to the matter in question; bold.
meanings.example: The impertinent tone adopted in correspondence with the regulator was itself cited in the subsequent enforcement review as evidence of poor governance culture.

meanings.definition: Not seeming reasonable or probable; failing to convince.
meanings.example: The explanation that the files had been misfiled rather than destroyed was considered implausible in light of the systematic pattern identified by the audit team.

meanings.definition: To create or perform spontaneously without preparation; to make something from whatever is available.
meanings.example: The team was forced to improvise when the agreed support materials failed to arrive, drawing on their collective experience to fill the gap.

meanings.definition: Tending to stir up conflict; an incendiary device; relating to fire.
meanings.example: The speech was widely condemned as incendiary, deploying language that those present recognised as a deliberate provocation to an already inflamed situation.

meanings.definition: Having no particular interest or concern; neither good nor bad; uncaring.
meanings.example: The institution had been indifferent to the warnings over many years, treating repeated concerns as minority views rather than indicators of a systemic problem.

meanings.definition: In or for the meantime; temporary; an intervening period.
meanings.example: An interim arrangement was put in place to maintain continuity of service while the permanent appointment was made through the normal process.

meanings.definition: To tear or deeply cut the flesh; to wound or distress deeply by harsh criticism.
meanings.example: The commentary lacerated the management team's credibility in a way that no subsequent statement of support from the board was able to fully repair.

meanings.definition: Lacking enthusiasm and determination; careless; languid.
meanings.example: The lackadaisical approach to data security that had characterised the organisation's culture for several years was the underlying cause of the breach.

meanings.definition: The quality of being more merciful or tolerant than expected; mildness of punishment.
meanings.example: The tribunal noted that leniency had been shown on two previous occasions and concluded that this context made a more serious response proportionate.

meanings.definition: The power to influence a person or situation; the ratio of a company's loan capital to its shares; to use borrowed capital.
meanings.example: The minority stakeholder had more leverage than its economic interest would suggest, because its agreement was required by the constitutional documents before any sale could proceed.

meanings.definition: The probability of something happening; the state of being likely.
meanings.example: The assessment concluded that the likelihood of compliance without formal enforcement was low, given the consistent pattern of behaviour over the preceding three years.

meanings.definition: Required by law or rules; compulsory; obligatory.
meanings.example: Attendance at the training was made mandatory for all those with decision-making authority, following the review's finding that inconsistent practice was rooted in a knowledge gap.

meanings.definition: To intervene in a dispute to bring about an agreement; to be a means of transmitting or conveying.
meanings.example: The former ambassador was asked to mediate in the dispute, a role she accepted on condition that both parties committed to the process in good faith.

meanings.definition: To make legally null and void; to make of no value or effect; to cancel out.
meanings.example: The subsequent disclosure nullified the earlier agreement, as it revealed information that one party had withheld at the time of signing.

meanings.definition: To take apart a machine to examine it and repair if necessary; to analyse and improve a system; to overtake.
meanings.example: A complete overhaul of the procurement process was recommended, as the review found systemic weaknesses that minor adjustments could not address.

meanings.definition: To reject or disallow the decision of someone in authority; to set aside a judgement.
meanings.example: The original decision was overruled on appeal, with the reviewing body finding that the reasoning had been inadequate rather than the conclusion necessarily wrong.

meanings.definition: Too enthusiastic or determined; excessively zealous; fanatical to an inappropriate degree.
meanings.example: The overzealous enforcement of the minor provision alienated users and generated reputational damage that far exceeded any harm the original rule was intended to prevent.

meanings.definition: To bring peace to a region; to quell the anger, agitation, or excitement of; to appease.
meanings.example: The concession was designed to pacify the most vocal opponents, buying time for a more comprehensive solution to be developed.

meanings.definition: To require as a precondition; to assume in advance; to take for granted.
meanings.example: The model presupposed a stable political environment, an assumption that was invalidated by events before the first phase of implementation had been completed.

meanings.definition: Attempting to impress by affecting greater importance, talent, or culture than one actually possesses; pompous.
meanings.example: The document was widely mocked as pretentious, substituting elaborate language for the clear and specific guidance that practitioners needed.

meanings.definition: To put an end to a rebellion or other disorder; to suppress or subdue; to allay fear or anxiety.
meanings.example: The firm statement from the leadership quelled the speculation that had been building for several days but left the underlying cause of the uncertainty unaddressed.

meanings.definition: To be repeated several times as an echo; to have continuing effects or repercussions.
meanings.example: The consequences of the decision continued to reverberate through the sector for years, affecting not only those directly involved but the regulatory environment as a whole.

meanings.definition: Not easily convinced; having doubts or reservations; inclined to question accepted opinions.
meanings.example: The initial response from analysts was sceptical, with most questioning whether the projected cost savings were achievable within the timescale stated.

meanings.definition: Conduct or speech inciting rebellion against the authority of a state or ruler.
meanings.example: The charge of sedition was considered extreme by many legal observers, who argued that the speech in question fell well within the protection afforded by existing free expression law.

meanings.definition: To cause to point in a different direction; to distort so as to be inaccurate; a distortion.
meanings.example: The sample design was found to skew the results in favour of those already engaged with the system, understating the experience of those who had disengaged.

meanings.definition: Not certain or fixed; hesitant or cautious in manner; provisional.
meanings.example: A tentative agreement was reached on the core principles, with both sides noting that the language of specific clauses remained to be finalised.

meanings.definition: Relating to the ownership of an area; protective of one's domain; relating to a territory.
meanings.example: The territorial disputes between the two agencies had their origins in a reorganisation that had left the boundaries of responsibility unclear.

meanings.definition: Lacking in courage or confidence; easily frightened; not bold.
