meanings.definition: A person who gives financial or other aid; a patron who supports a cause or institution.
meanings.example: An anonymous benefactor donated ten million pounds to establish the new cancer research centre.

meanings.definition: The state of being respected or admired; prestige conveyed by association.
meanings.example: Graduating from that law school carries enormous cachet in the legal profession.

meanings.definition: A distinguishing mark or seal on a document; a design on an envelope commemorating a postal event.
meanings.example: The first-day cover bore the official cachet of the commemorative stamp issue.

meanings.definition: The quality of being clear, logical, and convincing in argument or expression.
meanings.example: The cogency of her argument won over even the most sceptical members of the panel.

meanings.definition: A group of people with a shared characteristic, especially those born in the same year, or a group within a study.
meanings.example: The research tracked a cohort of 2,000 children from birth through to early adulthood.

meanings.definition: A companion or associate; a person who accompanies or supports another.
meanings.example: The senator arrived flanked by a cohort of aides and security personnel.

meanings.definition: The repetition of a process or utterance; a new version of something that incorporates improvements.
meanings.example: Each iteration of the software brought incremental improvements to the user interface.

meanings.definition: In mathematics and computing, the act of repeating a procedure applied to the result of a previous application.
meanings.example: The algorithm converges to a solution through successive iteration until the error falls below a threshold.

meanings.definition: An upward slope; a rising incline of ground.
meanings.example: The soldiers struggled up the steep acclivity under heavy fire, making slow and costly progress.

meanings.definition: Additional items of clothing or equipment used in a particular activity; accessories.
meanings.example: The mountaineer laid out all her accoutrements — ropes, crampons, harness — before the ascent.

meanings.definition: The willingness to take risks or act boldly in military or political affairs, often recklessly.
meanings.example: Critics accused the prime minister of adventurism for committing troops to a conflict with no clear exit strategy.

meanings.definition: The protection, backing, or support of a particular person or organisation.
meanings.example: The project was carried out under the aegis of the World Health Organization.

meanings.definition: In Greek mythology, the shield or breastplate of Zeus, later associated with Athena.
meanings.example: The warriors prayed for the aegis of Athena before marching into battle.

meanings.definition: A state of anxiety or nervous excitement; the act of disturbing or stirring up something.
meanings.example: There was visible agitation among the delegates as the results of the vote were announced.

meanings.definition: Campaigning or stirring up public concern about a political or social cause.
meanings.example: Decades of labour agitation ultimately secured the eight-hour working day.

meanings.definition: A minor change or addition designed to improve a text, piece of legislation, or constitution.
meanings.example: The First Amendment to the US Constitution enshrines the freedom of speech and religion.

meanings.definition: The process of making minor improvements to something.
meanings.example: The contract required several amendments before both parties were satisfied to sign.

meanings.definition: Lacking a moral sense; unconcerned with the rightness or wrongness of something.
meanings.example: The philosopher argued that corporations are inherently amoral entities driven solely by profit.

meanings.definition: A natural ability or talent; an inclination or tendency toward a particular activity.
meanings.example: Her aptitude for languages allowed her to master three foreign tongues before the age of twenty.

meanings.definition: A person who settles a dispute or has absolute authority in a matter; a judge.
meanings.example: The international court was called upon to serve as the final arbiter of the territorial dispute.

meanings.definition: A person whose views are accepted as authoritative on a particular subject, especially taste.
meanings.example: For decades, that critic was regarded as the arbiter of literary taste in the English-speaking world.

meanings.definition: To hesitate or be unwilling to accept an idea or undertaking; to refuse or recoil from something.
meanings.example: Many investors balked at the risk involved in financing such an untested technology.

meanings.definition: To obstruct or thwart someone in their efforts or plans.
meanings.example: The legislature balked the executive's attempt to push through emergency powers without scrutiny.

meanings.definition: Involving calamity; causing great distress or disaster; extremely unfortunate.
meanings.example: The calamitous flooding swept away entire villages and left thousands homeless.

meanings.definition: Skill in achieving one's ends by deceit or evasion; craftiness.
meanings.example: The fox's legendary cunning made it a recurring figure in fables about outfoxing larger adversaries.

meanings.definition: Ingenuity or skill (archaic or literary sense, without negative connotation).
meanings.example: With great cunning, the craftsman fashioned a lock so intricate that no key could replicate it.

meanings.definition: Moral or cultural decline, especially in a society or art form; luxurious self-indulgence.
meanings.example: The late Roman Empire is often cited as a case study in political decadence and institutional collapse.

meanings.definition: An artistic or literary movement in the late 19th century characterised by over-refinement and aestheticism.
meanings.example: Wilde was associated with the Decadence movement and its embrace of art for art's sake.

meanings.definition: To instruct or improve someone morally or intellectually; to enlighten.
meanings.example: The lecture series was designed not only to inform but to edify students on the ethical dimensions of medicine.

meanings.definition: The quality of being exact, precise, and accurate in every detail.
meanings.example: The engineering specifications were followed with exactitude, leaving no room for variation.

meanings.definition: Done with speed and efficiency; prompt and efficient in action.
meanings.example: The customs authority promised an expeditious review of the applications within three working days.

meanings.definition: The state or quality of being fierce, brutal, or violently intense.
meanings.example: The ferocity of the storm caught the coastal towns completely off guard.

meanings.definition: Extreme intensity or vehemence of an activity or feeling.
meanings.example: The ferocity of the debate in parliament reflected the depth of public feeling on the matter.

meanings.definition: To grow or develop in a healthy or vigorous way; to prosper or thrive.
meanings.example: Democracy can only flourish where civil liberties and an independent judiciary are protected.

meanings.definition: To wave something about to attract attention or make a dramatic gesture.
meanings.example: The conductor flourished his baton, signalling the orchestra to begin the finale.

meanings.definition: To prevent something from succeeding; to thwart a plan, attempt, or person.
meanings.example: Security services foiled an assassination attempt against the visiting head of state.

meanings.definition: As a noun: a person or thing that contrasts with and so enhances the qualities of another.
meanings.example: Watson serves as the perfect foil to Holmes, whose brilliance is thrown into sharper relief by contrast.

meanings.definition: A noisy disturbance or quarrel; a disorderly commotion or brawl.
meanings.example: A fracas broke out in the lobby of the hotel after the political meeting descended into argument.

meanings.definition: Brave, noble, or heroic in character; showing polite attention and respect, especially to women.
meanings.example: The gallant officer refused to leave the field until every one of his men had been evacuated.

meanings.definition: Magnificently dressed; dashing in appearance or behaviour.
meanings.example: He cut a gallant figure in his dress uniform as he led the regiment through the streets.

meanings.definition: The state of being inadvertent; failure to notice or act through carelessness or inattention.
meanings.example: The error was attributed to inadvertence rather than malice, and the apology was accepted.

meanings.definition: To bring oneself into favour with someone by flattery or obsequious behaviour.
meanings.example: He tried to ingratiate himself with the board by showering them with compliments at every meeting.

meanings.definition: A general dislike, distrust, or contempt of the human race or human society.
meanings.example: Years of professional disappointment had curdled his idealism into a deep and settled misanthropy.

meanings.definition: A small quantity of a particular thing, especially something desirable or valuable.
meanings.example: If you show even a modicum of effort, the team will rally behind you.

meanings.definition: A newly coined word or expression; the coining or use of new words.
meanings.example: The digital age has given rise to a torrent of neologisms, from "selfie" to "unfriend."

meanings.definition: The state of being unaware or unconscious of what is happening around one.
meanings.example: Exhausted after the battle, the soldiers fell into a deep and merciful oblivion.

meanings.definition: The state of being forgotten, especially by the public; the condition of extinction.
meanings.example: Many once-celebrated artists have faded into oblivion within a generation of their death.

meanings.definition: The practice of taking advantage of circumstances without regard for principle.
meanings.example: The merger was criticised as pure opportunism, exploiting the target company's moment of vulnerability.

meanings.definition: A person who delivers an oration; a skilled and persuasive public speaker.
meanings.example: Churchill was arguably the finest orator of the twentieth century, capable of galvanising a nation with words.

meanings.definition: Looking or sounding bizarre or unfamiliar; strikingly strange or unusual.
meanings.example: The architect's outlandish proposal to build a floating city drew ridicule from traditional engineers.

meanings.definition: A feeling of irritation or resentment resulting from a slight or indignity.
meanings.example: He resigned in a fit of pique after his proposal was rejected without discussion.

meanings.definition: As a verb: to stimulate or arouse interest, curiosity, or resentment.
meanings.example: The discovery of the sealed room piqued the curiosity of the archaeologists.

meanings.definition: Existing from the very beginning; primitive and ancient; fundamental to existence.
meanings.example: The primordial soup hypothesis suggests that life originated in a chemically rich ancient ocean.

meanings.definition: Of or relating to the most ancient time; not developed; elemental.
meanings.example: The forest retained a primordial quality, as though no human had ever set foot within it.

meanings.definition: A person or thing from which a person, animal, or plant is descended or originates; an ancestor.
meanings.example: Darwin identified the common progenitor from which all modern species eventually derived.

meanings.definition: The originator of a movement or artistic style; a precursor.
meanings.example: Ibsen is regarded as the progenitor of modern realistic drama.

meanings.definition: To appear or claim to be or do something, especially falsely; to allege.
meanings.example: The document purports to be a signed royal charter, but its authenticity has never been verified.

meanings.definition: As a noun: the meaning or substance of a document or speech.
meanings.example: The purport of the treaty was clear: neither party would engage in hostile acts for five years.

meanings.definition: Competition for the same objective or for superiority in the same field; the state of being rivals.
meanings.example: The intense rivalry between the two pharmaceutical companies accelerated the race for a vaccine.

meanings.definition: The use of humour, irony, exaggeration, or ridicule to expose and criticise people's stupidity or vices.
meanings.example: Swift's "A Modest Proposal" remains one of the most biting satires ever written in the English language.

meanings.definition: A play, novel, film, or other work that uses satire as its primary mode.
meanings.example: The television show was a brilliant satire of political life in Washington.

meanings.definition: Possessing supreme power or authority; completely independent and self-governing.
meanings.example: The court ruled that the island was a sovereign territory and not subject to external jurisdiction.

meanings.definition: Of the highest importance or quality; excellent.
meanings.example: Customer satisfaction was of sovereign importance to the company's long-term strategy.

meanings.definition: Exercising power in a cruel, arbitrary, or oppressive manner; despotic.
meanings.example: The tyrannical ruler silenced all dissent and ruled the country with brutal efficiency for thirty years.

meanings.definition: Punishment inflicted or retribution exacted for an injury or wrong; revenge.
meanings.example: Motivated by vengeance, the victim's family campaigned for decades to have the conviction overturned.

meanings.definition: Experienced or present to the maximum degree; utterly hopeless, miserable, or degrading.
meanings.example: The refugees lived in abject poverty, lacking even the most basic sanitation.

meanings.definition: Showing complete and utter submission; servile to a humiliating degree.
meanings.example: He offered an abject apology, prostrating himself before the board in hopes of retaining his position.

meanings.definition: The scope, extent, or bounds of something; the range within which something operates.
meanings.example: This matter falls outside the ambit of criminal law and must be addressed through civil proceedings.

meanings.definition: A very typical example of a certain person or thing; an original model.
meanings.example: Odysseus is the archetype of the cunning hero in Western literary tradition.

meanings.definition: In Jungian psychology, a primitive mental image inherited from ancestral experience and present in the collective unconscious.
meanings.example: The wise old man archetype appears across cultures as a figure of guidance and wisdom.

meanings.definition: Lack of equality or equivalence between parts or aspects of something; lack of symmetry.
meanings.example: The asymmetry in military capabilities between the two states made conventional deterrence untenable.

meanings.definition: A strong negative reaction by a large number of people to something, especially a social or political change.
meanings.example: The proposed tax on carbon emissions triggered a fierce backlash from industry groups.

meanings.definition: Positioned in or relating to the sky or outer space; supremely good or beautiful.
meanings.example: The astronomer spent decades mapping celestial bodies in the outer reaches of the solar system.

meanings.definition: Of or relating to heaven; divine or supremely excellent.
meanings.example: The choir's celestial harmonies filled the cathedral and moved the congregation to tears.

meanings.definition: A deep fissure in the earth, rock, or another surface; a profound difference between people.
meanings.example: A yawning chasm had opened in the bedrock, making any further excavation dangerous.

meanings.definition: A wide difference in interests, opinions, or circumstances between two parties.
meanings.example: The chasm between the two factions proved unbridgeable despite months of negotiation.

meanings.definition: A fortress, typically on high ground, protecting or dominating a city; a last refuge.
meanings.example: The old citadel still loomed over the town, a reminder of its medieval defensive purpose.

meanings.definition: A place of safety; a stronghold of a doctrine or interest.
meanings.example: The central bank was seen as the last citadel of economic orthodoxy in an era of radical fiscal policy.

meanings.definition: The act or process of making things equal in measure or proportion; equating values for comparison.
meanings.example: The commensuration of disparate risk factors into a single index was itself a contentious methodological choice.

meanings.definition: A continuous sequence in which adjacent elements are not perceptibly different from each other, though the extremes are quite different.
meanings.example: Autism is now understood as a spectrum — a continuum of traits rather than a binary condition.

meanings.definition: A detailed analysis and assessment of something, especially a literary, philosophical, or political theory.
meanings.example: Her critique of the government's housing policy identified three fundamental structural flaws.

meanings.definition: A ceramic or metal container in which metals or other substances may be melted or subjected to high temperature.
meanings.example: The alchemist heated the crucible until the metal inside turned to liquid.

meanings.definition: A place or situation in which different elements interact to produce something new; a severe trial.
meanings.example: The battlefield proved a crucible in which ordinary soldiers were forged into seasoned veterans.

meanings.definition: To utter or deliver words or a speech in a rhetorical or impassioned way; to recite dramatically.
meanings.example: He declaimed the poem with theatrical intensity, projecting to the back of the auditorium.

meanings.definition: To protest strongly against or attack verbally; to rail against.
meanings.example: The senator declaimed against the proposed legislation as a threat to individual liberty.

meanings.definition: To abandon one's country or cause in favour of an opposing one.
meanings.example: The intelligence officer defected to the West, taking with him a trove of classified documents.

meanings.definition: To diminish gradually in size, amount, or strength.
meanings.example: The party's support dwindled over the decade as younger voters abandoned it in favour of alternatives.

meanings.definition: A level or rank in an organisation, a profession, or society.
meanings.example: She had risen through the lower echelons of the civil service to reach a senior management role.

meanings.definition: A formation of troops, aircraft, or ships in parallel rows, each one to the side and behind the one in front.
meanings.example: The aircraft flew in echelon formation, each plane slightly behind and to the right of the previous.

meanings.definition: A person or thing that is mysterious, puzzling, or difficult to understand.
meanings.example: The Sphinx remained an enigma to ancient travellers, who could not decipher the meaning of her riddle.

meanings.definition: A person or thing that is a perfect example of a particular quality or type; a summary.
meanings.example: She was considered the epitome of professional composure, never visibly rattled in a crisis.

meanings.definition: To adopt or support a cause, belief, or way of life.
meanings.example: He espoused radical economic theories that were far outside the mainstream at the time.

meanings.definition: To strengthen a place with defensive works; to make stronger or more resistant.
meanings.example: The garrison worked through the night to fortify their position before the expected assault.

meanings.definition: To invigorate or encourage someone; to strengthen one's resolve or physical resilience.
meanings.example: A brisk walk and a strong coffee were enough to fortify her for the long day of meetings ahead.

meanings.definition: The action or process of innovating; a new method, idea, product, or process.
meanings.example: The company's culture of innovation allowed it to anticipate market shifts and disrupt established industries.

meanings.definition: Necessary to make a whole complete; fundamental or essential.
meanings.example: Trust is an integral component of any effective therapeutic relationship.

meanings.definition: Of or relating to a mathematical integral or integration.
meanings.example: The integral calculus developed by Newton and Leibniz transformed the study of continuous change.

meanings.definition: To come between so as to prevent or alter a result or course of events.
meanings.example: The international community was slow to intervene in the conflict, allowing the situation to deteriorate.

meanings.definition: To occur in time between events; to interrupt.
meanings.example: Unforeseen circumstances intervened, forcing the delegation to postpone its visit.

meanings.definition: Emitting light not caused by incandescence, especially at low temperatures.
meanings.example: The deep-sea organism was luminescent, its body casting an eerie blue glow in the darkness.

meanings.definition: Lack of variety; tedious sameness or uniformity.
meanings.example: The monotony of the assembly line work drove many employees to seek more stimulating employment.

meanings.definition: Having unlimited or very great power; all-powerful.
meanings.example: The ancient gods were portrayed as omnipotent beings whose whims could reshape the natural world.

meanings.definition: Resembling or fit for a palace; spacious and splendid.
meanings.example: The chief executive occupied a palatial corner office with sweeping views of the city.

meanings.definition: Relating to or caused by disease; compulsive or unreasonable.
meanings.example: The patient was diagnosed with a pathological condition requiring immediate surgical intervention.

meanings.definition: Compulsive and repetitive in a manner beyond rational control.
meanings.example: His pathological need for approval made it impossible for him to take decisions independently.

meanings.definition: The desire to promote the welfare of others, expressed through generous donations of money or time.
meanings.example: Her philanthropy endowed hospitals, universities, and cultural institutions across three continents.

meanings.definition: To be a sign or warning that something, especially something momentous or calamitous, is likely to happen.
meanings.example: The sudden drop in consumer confidence portended a deeper economic recession than the government anticipated.

meanings.definition: A person, especially a young one, endowed with exceptional qualities or abilities.
meanings.example: The child prodigy had composed a symphony before the age of twelve.

meanings.definition: An impressive or outstanding example of a particular quality.
meanings.example: The bridge is an engineering prodigy, built with no modern machinery in record time.

meanings.definition: An accusation in response to an accusation made by someone else; mutual blame.
meanings.example: The post-election period was marked by bitter recriminations between the two campaign teams.

meanings.definition: Causing general public outrage by a perceived gross violation of morality or law.
meanings.example: The minister's scandalous conduct forced his resignation within twenty-four hours of the revelations.

meanings.definition: Containing damaging and false information about someone.
meanings.example: The tabloid published a scandalous story that had no basis in documented fact.

meanings.definition: Exceptionally good or great; remarkable; denoting a single person or thing.
meanings.example: Her singular ability to synthesise disparate research streams set her apart from her peers.

meanings.definition: Less important than or serving the needs of something or someone; obsequiously compliant.
meanings.example: A free press cannot be subservient to government interests if it is to fulfil its democratic function.

meanings.definition: Relating to or denoting a region or climate characterised by mild temperatures; showing moderation or self-restraint.
meanings.example: The temperate climate of the Pacific Northwest supports a diverse range of agricultural crops.

meanings.definition: Showing moderation or self-restraint; calm and reasonable in behaviour.
meanings.example: Despite the provocation, the negotiator maintained a temperate tone throughout the difficult discussions.

meanings.definition: The fact of appearing everywhere or of being very widespread.
meanings.example: The ubiquity of smartphones has transformed the way people access information and communicate.

meanings.definition: To find fault with or reproach someone severely.
meanings.example: The general upbraided his officers for their failure to hold the position, calling it a tactical disgrace.

meanings.definition: The action of taking a position of power or importance illegally or by force.
meanings.example: The coup was widely condemned as an usurpation of democratic authority by the military.

meanings.definition: To defeat thoroughly in battle or in a contest; to overcome completely.
meanings.example: Alexander vanquished his Persian adversaries at the Battle of Gaugamela in 331 BC.

meanings.definition: A person who is attracted to foreign peoples, manners, or cultures.
meanings.example: As a xenophile, she spent every holiday in a different country, immersing herself in local customs.

meanings.definition: Inspiring disgust and loathing; repugnant.
meanings.example: The conditions in the detention facility were described by inspectors as abhorrent and inhumane.

meanings.definition: A cause of pain or harm; a state of pain, distress, or hardship.
meanings.example: Poverty remained the greatest affliction facing rural communities in the region.

meanings.definition: Into separate parts; apart; into pieces.
meanings.example: The explosion tore the bridge asunder, scattering debris across a wide area.

meanings.definition: Many and of various types; having many varied parts or aspects.
meanings.example: The commission's multifarious responsibilities ranged from financial regulation to consumer protection.

meanings.definition: Noticeable or prominent in an unwelcome or intrusive way.
meanings.example: The obtrusive signage detracted from the natural beauty of the national park.

meanings.definition: Knowing everything; having complete or unlimited knowledge.
meanings.example: The novel is narrated by an omniscient narrator who has access to every character's innermost thoughts.

meanings.definition: Extreme unwillingness to spend money or use resources; extreme frugality.
meanings.example: The council's parsimony in funding public services drew criticism from residents and charities alike.

meanings.definition: In science, the principle that the simplest explanation is to be preferred (Occam's razor).
meanings.example: The principle of parsimony guided the researchers toward the simplest model that fitted the data.

meanings.definition: A trace or remnant of something that is disappearing or no longer exists.
meanings.example: The abandoned railway station was the last vestige of the town's industrial heritage.

meanings.definition: Having or showing a feeling of vague or regretful longing.
meanings.example: She gave the old house one last wistful glance before driving away for the final time.

meanings.definition: A state of utter hopelessness, misery, or degradation; the condition of being cast down or humiliated.
meanings.example: The documentary captured the abjection of life in the informal settlements, where basic services were absent.

meanings.definition: A deep or seemingly bottomless chasm; an immeasurably deep gulf or pit.
meanings.example: The climbers peered over the edge into the abyss and decided the descent was too dangerous.

meanings.definition: A situation or feeling that appears to have no end or resolution; the depths of something negative.
meanings.example: After the financial crash, the country teetered on the abyss of economic collapse.

meanings.definition: Public support for or recommendation of a particular cause or policy.
meanings.example: Her decades of advocacy on behalf of refugees influenced legislation in multiple countries.

meanings.definition: A set of principles underlying the work of a particular artist or artistic movement; concerned with beauty or the appreciation of beauty.
meanings.example: The architect's aesthetic was defined by the interplay of light, space, and natural materials.

meanings.definition: The medieval forerunner of chemistry, seeking to transmute base metals into gold; any mysterious process of transformation.
meanings.example: There is an alchemy to great leadership: the ability to transform disparate individuals into a cohesive team.

meanings.definition: A friendly relationship; peaceful harmony between people or groups.
meanings.example: The two nations had maintained a relationship of amity and mutual respect for over a century.

meanings.definition: A short and amusing or interesting story about a real incident or person.
meanings.example: He illustrated his lecture with anecdotes drawn from his years working in conflict zones.

meanings.definition: The action of taking something for one's own use, typically without the owner's permission.
meanings.example: The legal dispute centred on the alleged appropriation of intellectual property by a competitor.

meanings.definition: The action of a legislature in setting aside funds for a specific purpose.
meanings.example: The budget appropriation for infrastructure was the largest in the department's history.

meanings.definition: To ask someone urgently and fervently to do something; to implore.
meanings.example: The prisoners beseeched the judge to show leniency, citing their families' dependence on them.

meanings.definition: Lacking vegetation and exposed to the elements; cold and desolate.
meanings.example: The bleak moorland stretched to the horizon, broken only by a few stunted trees.

meanings.definition: Offering little hope or prospect of improvement; depressingly bare.
meanings.example: The report painted a bleak picture of the country's economic prospects for the coming decade.

meanings.definition: A particular way or method of doing something; especially a criminal's characteristic methods of working.
meanings.example: The detective analysed the crime scene to identify the killer's modus operandi.

meanings.definition: An essential condition; a thing that is absolutely necessary.
meanings.example: A commitment to transparency is the sine qua non of any credible anti-corruption initiative.

meanings.definition: The existing state of affairs, especially regarding social or political issues.
meanings.example: The radical faction sought to challenge the status quo, while moderates urged caution.

meanings.definition: A thing that has already happened or been decided and is now unalterable.
meanings.example: By the time parliament was informed, the merger had become a fait accompli.

meanings.definition: Created or done for a particular purpose as necessary, rather than planned in advance.
meanings.example: An ad hoc committee was formed to deal with the unprecedented crisis.

meanings.definition: Complete freedom to act as one wishes or thinks best.
meanings.example: The new director was given carte blanche to restructure the department as he saw fit.

meanings.definition: A policy or attitude of leaving things to take their own course, without interfering; free-market policy.
meanings.example: The government's laissez-faire approach to the economy left businesses free to operate with minimal regulation.

meanings.definition: A favour or advantage granted or expected in return for something.
meanings.example: The diplomatic arrangement was widely seen as a quid pro quo: investment in exchange for political support.

meanings.definition: An act or situation provoking or justifying war; a pretext for conflict.
meanings.example: The assassination provided the regime with a casus belli to launch its long-planned military campaign.

meanings.definition: Not easily upset or excited; calm and peaceful; not prone to disturbance.
meanings.example: The lake lay placid in the early morning light, its surface unmarked by any breeze.

meanings.definition: To tidy or groom oneself or plumage with great care; to congratulate oneself with smug self-satisfaction.
meanings.example: He spent ten minutes preening in the mirror before the awards ceremony.

meanings.definition: To be a sign or warning of a future event; to portend.
meanings.example: The sudden silence in the forest seemed to presage something dangerous.

meanings.definition: Produced or existing in very large amounts; plentiful or overly abundant.
meanings.example: She offered profuse apologies for the mix-up, embarrassed by the confusion she had caused.

meanings.definition: Tending or able to change frequently or easily; capable of assuming many forms.
meanings.example: His protean talents allowed him to move between roles as novelist, screenwriter, and stage director.

meanings.definition: Aggressive greed; voracious desire for money, possessions, or other things.
meanings.example: The rapacity of the colonial administration stripped the region of its natural resources.

meanings.definition: To act servilely to someone in a position of power; to fawn obsequiously.
meanings.example: He toadied to the managing director at every opportunity, hoping to secure a promotion.

meanings.definition: A cause of great trouble or suffering; a state of great distress.
meanings.example: The family endured years of tribulation before circumstances finally improved.

meanings.definition: Of language or style: pompous and over-elaborate; inflated; bombastic.
meanings.example: The committee report was criticised for its turgid prose, which obscured rather than communicated its findings.

meanings.definition: Swollen and distended; of water: swollen or turbid.
meanings.example: The turgid river had overflowed its banks, flooding the lowland fields.

meanings.definition: The formal process by which an accused person is called before a court to be informed of and respond to charges.
meanings.example: The arraignment was scheduled for the following Monday, at which point the defendant would enter a plea.

meanings.definition: Evidence that confirms or supports a statement, theory, or finding.
meanings.example: The prosecution's case relied on documentary corroboration of the witness's testimony.

meanings.definition: The process of giving sworn evidence; a formal statement to be used as evidence in court.
meanings.example: The deposition of the key witness lasted three days and covered every aspect of the transaction.

meanings.definition: The action of deposing someone from power.
meanings.example: The military's deposition of the elected president provoked widespread international condemnation.

meanings.definition: To say or pronounce words clearly; to articulate distinctly.
meanings.example: The speech therapist worked with patients to help them enunciate more clearly after the stroke.

meanings.definition: To set forth or state a theory, principle, or plan precisely and clearly.
meanings.example: The manifesto enunciated three core principles that would guide the party's policy agenda.

meanings.definition: The fact of no longer being on friendly terms or part of a social group; emotional distance.
meanings.example: Years of estrangement from his family left him with a sense of isolation he could not overcome.

meanings.definition: To hand over a person accused or convicted of a crime to the jurisdiction of another country.
meanings.example: The suspect was extradited from Switzerland to face charges of financial fraud in the United States.

meanings.definition: Resembling or appropriate to a farce; absurd, ridiculous, or ludicrously impractical.
meanings.example: The entire trial had descended into something farcical by the third day of procedural mishaps.

meanings.definition: To give a servile display of exaggerated flattery or affection; to cringe and behave submissively.
meanings.example: She was irritated by the way junior colleagues would fawn over the director at every staff meeting.

meanings.definition: A prolonged and bitter quarrel or dispute; a state of persistent mutual hostility.
meanings.example: The feud between the two families had lasted for generations, rooted in a disputed inheritance.

meanings.definition: A feeling that something bad will happen; fearful apprehension.
meanings.example: She boarded the train with a sense of foreboding that she could not entirely explain.

meanings.definition: Fast and energetic in a rather wild and uncontrolled way; frenzied.
meanings.example: The frenetic pace of trading in the final minutes before market close caused a sharp spike in volatility.

meanings.definition: In chess, an opening move involving a sacrifice of a piece; more broadly, a device or trick to gain an advantage.
meanings.example: The prime minister's resignation was widely seen as a political gambit designed to force early elections.

meanings.definition: The process of carrying or being carried in the womb between conception and birth; the development period.
meanings.example: Human gestation typically lasts approximately forty weeks from conception to birth.

meanings.definition: The development of something over time, especially an idea or plan.
meanings.example: The novel was the result of a long gestation; the author had carried the ideas for over a decade.

meanings.definition: An excessively abundant supply of something; a surfeit.
meanings.example: A global glut of crude oil caused prices to plummet to levels not seen in decades.

meanings.definition: A religious discourse delivered as part of a church service; a sermon.
meanings.example: The bishop delivered a homily on the theme of reconciliation that moved many in the congregation.

meanings.definition: A tedious moralising lecture or discourse.
meanings.example: His colleagues had grown weary of the director's homily on workplace discipline.

meanings.definition: To allow oneself to enjoy the pleasure of something; to gratify or yield to a desire or wish.
meanings.example: After years of strict dieting, she allowed herself to indulge in a slice of cake at the celebration.

meanings.definition: To allow someone to do or have whatever they want, even if it is not beneficial.
meanings.example: His parents had always indulged his every whim, leaving him ill-equipped for the demands of adult life.

meanings.definition: A change in the pitch or tone of a voice; a modulation in speech.
meanings.example: The actor used subtle changes in inflection to convey the character's shifting emotional state.

meanings.definition: The modification of a word to express different grammatical categories such as tense, number, or case.
meanings.example: Latin is a highly inflected language with extensive inflection in its noun paradigms.

meanings.definition: To make something appear larger than it is; to increase the apparent size of an object.
meanings.example: The microscope magnified the sample to a thousand times its actual size.

meanings.definition: To exaggerate the importance or seriousness of something.
meanings.example: The media tended to magnify every setback, creating undue public alarm.

meanings.definition: Dislike of, contempt for, or ingrained prejudice against women.
meanings.example: The study documented endemic misogyny in the workplace, manifesting as systematic bias in hiring decisions.

meanings.definition: Of great importance or significance, especially in its bearing on the future.
meanings.example: The signing of the peace treaty was described as the most momentous event in the region's history.

meanings.definition: Living or moving from place to place without a fixed home; of or relating to nomads.
meanings.example: Nomadic herders in Central Asia follow seasonal migration routes that have remained unchanged for millennia.

meanings.definition: The formation of a word by imitation of a sound associated with the thing it names; the use of such words.
meanings.example: Words such as "buzz," "hiss," and "clang" are examples of onomatopoeia in English.

meanings.definition: Extremely bad; appalling.
meanings.example: The customer service was abysmal — calls went unanswered for days.

meanings.definition: The quality of being bitter and sharp in manner, especially in speech; the state of being acrimonious.
meanings.example: The acrimoniousness of the debate shocked observers who had expected a collegial exchange.

meanings.definition: The belief that it is impossible to know whether God exists; more broadly, uncertainty about a proposition.
meanings.example: His agnosticism was not indifference but a reasoned refusal to claim certainty where none was available.

meanings.definition: Containing or relating to allegory; constituting or using symbolic representation.
meanings.example: The novel's allegorical dimension becomes clear when the protagonist's journey is read as a commentary on social inequality.

meanings.definition: Able to use either hand equally well; metaphorically, able to function well in two different areas.
meanings.example: The rare ambidextrous pitcher could switch throwing arms depending on the batter's dominant side.

meanings.definition: Regarding humans as the central element of existence; interpreting the world in terms of human values.
meanings.example: Critics argued that the anthropocentric worldview had led to the systematic degradation of natural ecosystems.

meanings.definition: The attribution of human characteristics to non-human entities, especially animals or deities.
meanings.example: The tendency towards anthropomorphism explains why people often believe their pets understand complex emotions.

meanings.definition: Relating to the study or collection of antiquities; concerned with old, historical objects.
meanings.example: Her antiquarian interests led her to collect rare manuscripts from the sixteenth century.

meanings.definition: Excessively concerned with the past; old-fashioned in orientation.
meanings.example: Critics found his antiquarian approach to legal interpretation unsuitable for a rapidly changing world.

meanings.definition: An opening, hole, or gap; especially the opening in a camera lens controlling light entering.
meanings.example: The photographer adjusted the aperture to allow more light and achieve a shallower depth of field.

meanings.definition: A space through which light may enter; a gap or breach.
meanings.example: A small aperture in the cave ceiling let in a single shaft of sunlight.

meanings.definition: Relating to or resembling an apocalypse; portending utter devastation or doom.
meanings.example: Apocalyptic warnings about the consequences of inaction failed to mobilise sufficient political will.

meanings.definition: Of or resembling the biblical Book of Revelation; prophetic.
meanings.example: The artist's apocalyptic imagery drew on both religious tradition and contemporary environmental anxiety.

meanings.definition: A word, expression, or usage no longer in common use but retained in special contexts; the use of archaic forms.
meanings.example: The legal contract was peppered with archaisms such as "hereinafter" and "aforesaid".

meanings.definition: The process or art of forming reasons and presenting them to prove or disprove a proposition; debate or discussion.
meanings.example: Strong argumentation requires clear logical structure, credible evidence, and awareness of counterarguments.

meanings.definition: Made using traditional, non-mechanised methods by skilled craftspeople; relating to artisans.
meanings.example: The market featured artisanal cheeses, handmade ceramics, and small-batch preserves from local producers.

meanings.definition: Causing the contraction of body tissues; sharp or severe in manner or style.
meanings.example: The astringent tannins in red wine create a dry, puckering sensation in the mouth.

meanings.definition: Sharply incisive, critical, or severe in commentary.
meanings.example: Her astringent wit made her columns entertaining but also earned her many critics.

meanings.definition: Approaching a value or curve arbitrarily closely without ever reaching it; relating to an asymptote.
meanings.example: Economic progress in the region had been asymptotic — always nearing but never quite reaching the living standards of developed nations.

meanings.definition: Aggressiveness or eagerness to fight; the quality of being bellicose.
meanings.example: The government's bellicosity alarmed neighbouring states, who feared a military confrontation.

meanings.definition: The ability to speak and understand two languages fluently; the policy of promoting two languages in a society.
meanings.example: Childhood bilingualism has been associated with cognitive advantages including improved executive function.

meanings.definition: To talk at length in a pompous or self-important way; to speechify.
meanings.example: Politicians frequently bloviate on television, saying very little in a very long time.

meanings.definition: Exercising a compelling charm that inspires devotion in others; relating to a form of Christianity emphasising supernatural gifts.
meanings.example: A charismatic leader can mobilise people around a vision that would otherwise seem impossible to achieve.

meanings.definition: Willingness to please others or to accept their wishes; compliance and obligingness.
meanings.example: Her complaisance in meetings masked a keen critical intelligence that emerged only in private conversations.

meanings.definition: Pleasing to the ear; having an agreeable sound; melodious.
meanings.example: The soprano's voice was remarkably euphonious, each note floating effortlessly above the orchestra.

meanings.definition: Lack of experience, wisdom, or judgement; innocent simplicity; ingenuousness.
meanings.example: The naïveté of the new recruit was apparent in his assumption that every colleague acted in good faith.

meanings.definition: Like a labyrinth; intricate and confusing; having many winding passages.
meanings.example: The labyrinthine bureaucracy made obtaining a simple permit an ordeal lasting several months.

meanings.definition: A tendency to excessive and often incoherent talkativeness; a flow of words.
meanings.example: The professor's logorrhoea was legendary; a simple question about the reading list could spark a two-hour digression.

meanings.definition: A person who inspires or influences others; a leading light in a particular field.
meanings.example: The conference brought together luminaries from physics, biology, and computer science to discuss the frontiers of science.

meanings.definition: To engage in plots or secret schemes; to conspire.
meanings.example: The dissident factions had been machinating for months before the coup attempt was finally discovered.

meanings.definition: The intention or desire to do evil; ill will; a state of mind that causes one to do wrong.
meanings.example: The prosecution argued that the defendant had acted with malice, deliberately targeting the victim over a personal grudge.

meanings.definition: A device such as a pattern of letters or associations that assists in remembering something.
meanings.example: "Every Good Boy Deserves Football" is a mnemonic used to remember the notes on the lines of a treble clef.

meanings.definition: Noisy and difficult to control; aggressively boisterous.
meanings.example: The obstreperous crowd at the public meeting made it impossible for the speakers to be heard.

meanings.definition: A person who writes or speaks with strong controversial opinions; an aggressive debater.
meanings.example: As a polemicist, she attracted fierce opposition but also won a devoted following among readers who shared her convictions.

meanings.definition: An expression of praise or admiration; an award or privilege granted as a special honour.
meanings.example: The film received accolades at every major festival, culminating in three Academy Award nominations.

meanings.definition: To comment on issues of right and wrong, typically with an attitude of moral superiority.
meanings.example: The column had a tendency to moralize, which alienated readers who resented being lectured.

meanings.definition: An organism that lives in or on another organism and benefits at its host's expense.
meanings.example: The tapeworm is a parasite that can inhabit the human intestinal tract for years without being detected.

meanings.definition: A person who exploits others and gives nothing in return.
meanings.example: He was dismissed as a parasite who had used the charity for personal gain.

meanings.definition: A natural object or animal believed by a particular society to have spiritual significance; an emblem or symbol.
meanings.example: For many Indigenous communities, the totem animal represents a spiritual relationship with nature and ancestry.

meanings.definition: Burning fiercely; filled with bright light or strong emotion.
meanings.example: By the time the fire crews arrived, the warehouse was ablaze, flames visible from miles away.

meanings.definition: Unbreakable; impossible to penetrate or resist; extremely hard.
meanings.example: Her adamantine resolve in the face of pressure earned her the respect even of her opponents.

meanings.definition: A person who selflessly serves the welfare of others; someone motivated by altruism.
meanings.example: She was regarded as a genuine altruist who donated a significant portion of her income to charitable causes.

meanings.definition: Characterised by the use of aphorisms; expressing truths concisely and memorably.
meanings.example: His writing style was wonderfully aphoristic, each sentence capable of standing alone as a reflection on human nature.

meanings.definition: Relating to or constituting an archive; preserved for historical reference.
meanings.example: The documentary was enriched by archival footage that had not been publicly screened for fifty years.

meanings.definition: An extreme preoccupation with collecting and possessing books; obsessive book collecting.
meanings.example: His bibliomania had filled every room of the house with towers of volumes, many of which he had never read.

meanings.definition: The state of having two poles or extremes; in psychology, relating to bipolar disorder.
meanings.example: The bipolarity of the Cold War era shaped global alliances for more than four decades.

meanings.definition: Moving or tending to move towards a centre; directed inward.
meanings.example: The centripetal force keeping the satellite in orbit is provided by Earth's gravitational pull.

meanings.definition: Tending to bring together or unify; consolidating.
meanings.example: Economic integration had a centripetal effect on the region, drawing its member states into ever-closer cooperation.

meanings.definition: Love, loyalty, or enthusiasm for a person, activity, or cause; religious worship or observance.
meanings.example: Her devotion to her craft was absolute: she practised for eight hours a day for thirty years.

meanings.definition: Relating to or resembling an epigram; concisely and cleverly expressed.
meanings.example: Oscar Wilde's epigrammatic style made even his most trenchant social criticisms sound like witticisms.

meanings.definition: Harsh criticism; the act of denouncing or censuring severely.
meanings.example: The review was a thorough excoriation of the government's handling of the crisis, sparing no department.

meanings.definition: The removal of skin by abrasion; a raw place on the skin.
meanings.example: The doctor noted an excoriation on the patient's arm, consistent with contact with rough material.

meanings.definition: To analyse and develop an idea or principle in detail; to make something clear.
meanings.example: The philosopher spent three chapters attempting to explicate the distinction between liberty and licence.

meanings.definition: The state of being divided into self-interested groups within a larger organisation; internal factional rivalry.
meanings.example: Factionalism within the party had paralysed its ability to respond coherently to the crisis.

meanings.definition: Excessive enthusiasm or zeal, especially in religion or politics; extreme and intolerant devotion.
meanings.example: The historian traced the roots of the movement's fanaticism to a sense of collective grievance and disenfranchisement.

meanings.definition: Having or characterised by a flaw or flaws; imperfect or defective.
meanings.example: The study's conclusions were compelling but built on flawed methodology that critics were quick to identify.

meanings.definition: The complete range or scope of something; all the notes in a musical scale.
meanings.example: The exhibition ran the full gamut of human experience, from birth to death, through works spanning five centuries.

meanings.definition: Habitual greed or excess in eating and drinking; one of the seven deadly sins.
meanings.example: Medieval moralists considered gluttony not merely a physical failing but a symptom of spiritual disorder.

meanings.definition: The quality or state of being homogeneous; uniformity or similarity of composition.
meanings.example: Critics of globalisation worried that it would reduce cultural homogeneity, erasing local traditions.

meanings.definition: From the beginning; used in law to indicate a contract or legal act that is void from its inception.
meanings.example: The contract was declared void ab initio because one party had lacked the legal capacity to enter into it.

meanings.definition: By that very fact or act; as an inevitable consequence.
meanings.example: Declaring bankruptcy does not ipso facto resolve all of a company's debts; many obligations survive insolvency.

meanings.definition: Denoting work undertaken voluntarily and without payment, especially legal work for clients who cannot afford fees.
meanings.example: The law firm dedicated ten per cent of its billing hours to pro bono representation of asylum seekers.

meanings.definition: Under judicial consideration; relating to a case that is currently being considered by a court and therefore not open to public discussion.
meanings.example: The minister refused to comment on the matter, noting that it was currently sub judice.

meanings.definition: Among other things; used when providing examples from a larger set.
meanings.example: The report examined, inter alia, the impact of interest rates on housing affordability and business investment.

meanings.definition: Actually existing in fact, though not formally or legally established.
meanings.example: She had become the de facto leader of the organisation despite holding no official title.

meanings.definition: By right or according to law; existing by legal right or formal establishment.
meanings.example: The government sought international recognition as the de jure sovereign authority over the disputed territory.

meanings.definition: The legal principle that no one should be tried or punished twice for the same offence; double jeopardy.
meanings.example: Defence counsel invoked the principle of non bis in idem to have the second prosecution dismissed.

meanings.definition: The use of more words than are necessary to express meaning; redundant phrasing.
meanings.example: "Free gift" is a pleonasm because all gifts are by definition free.

meanings.definition: The doctrine that knowledge, truth, and morality are not absolute but exist in relation to culture, society, or historical context.
meanings.example: Critics of moral relativism argue that it cannot account for universal human rights.

meanings.definition: A philosophical system holding that every rationally justifiable assertion can be scientifically verified; the approach to knowledge that accepts only empirically observable facts.
meanings.example: The influence of positivism on twentieth-century social science was profound, encouraging rigorous quantitative methods.

meanings.definition: The gradual reduction of a debt by making regular payments of principal and interest; the writing off of an asset over time.
meanings.example: The amortisation schedule showed that the mortgage would be fully paid off in twenty-five years.

meanings.definition: The branch of economics concerned with large-scale or general economic factors, such as national productivity, trade balances, and monetary policy.
meanings.example: The professor taught macroeconomics to undergraduates, focusing on fiscal policy and the business cycle.

meanings.definition: The branch of economics concerned with individual firms, households, and markets, and their decision-making processes.
meanings.example: Microeconomics provides tools for understanding how prices are determined and how consumers and producers respond to incentives.

meanings.definition: Relating to government revenue, especially taxes; relating to financial matters more generally.
meanings.example: The government's fiscal policy involved cutting spending while maintaining investment in infrastructure.

meanings.definition: Relating to money or currency; relating to the supply of money in an economy.
meanings.example: The central bank's monetary policy involved raising interest rates to control inflation.

meanings.definition: The practice of setting prices so low as to drive competitors out of the market, with the intention of raising prices once dominance is achieved.
meanings.example: Regulators investigated the company for predatory pricing after smaller rivals reported being undercut below cost.

meanings.definition: An association of manufacturers or suppliers with the purpose of maintaining high prices and restricting competition.
meanings.example: The pharmaceutical cartel was fined billions of euros for price-fixing across multiple European markets.

meanings.definition: An association of criminal organisations, especially drug traffickers, that coordinates activities.
meanings.example: Law enforcement agencies collaborated across three continents to dismantle the drug cartel.

meanings.definition: A situation in which two suppliers dominate a market; by extension, any situation dominated by two powerful entities.
meanings.example: The software market had become a duopoly, with two companies controlling over eighty per cent of enterprise licences.

meanings.definition: The tendency to search for, interpret, and remember information in a way that confirms one's pre-existing beliefs.
meanings.example: The study found that confirmation bias led investors to overweight positive news about their existing holdings.

meanings.definition: The act of manipulating someone by psychological means into questioning their own sanity or perception of reality.
meanings.example: The therapist recognised the classic signs of gaslighting in the client's description of her relationship.

meanings.definition: The tendency to follow the crowd or adopt group behaviour without independent thinking.
meanings.example: Financial bubbles are often driven by herd mentality, as investors follow each other rather than conduct independent analysis.

meanings.definition: Attitudes or stereotypes that affect decisions and actions unconsciously, without the individual being aware of their influence.
meanings.example: Studies on implicit bias show that even well-meaning people can make discriminatory decisions based on unconscious assumptions.

meanings.definition: The persistent internal experience of believing oneself to be a fraud despite evident success or competence.
meanings.example: Despite her numerous awards, she suffered from impostor syndrome and feared being exposed as unworthy of her position.

meanings.definition: The tendency within a group to reach consensus without critical evaluation, suppressing dissenting views to maintain harmony.
meanings.example: The committee's decision had all the hallmarks of groupthink: no one had raised objections until after the disaster.

meanings.definition: A process or set of rules followed in calculations or problem-solving operations, especially by a computer.
meanings.example: The search engine's algorithm assigns rankings based on hundreds of factors including relevance and credibility.

meanings.definition: Any systematic process for solving a problem or achieving an outcome.
meanings.example: Critics argued that the bank's lending algorithm was discriminatory in its effect on minority applicants.

meanings.definition: To cause or accelerate a chemical reaction by acting as a catalyst.
meanings.example: The enzyme catalyses the breakdown of starch into simpler sugars during digestion.

meanings.definition: To precipitate or speed up a process, especially a social or political one.
meanings.example: The financial crisis catalysed a fundamental rethinking of regulatory frameworks across the developed world.

meanings.definition: A machine using centrifugal force to separate substances of different densities; used widely in laboratory and industrial settings.
meanings.example: Blood samples are placed in a centrifuge to separate plasma from red blood cells.

meanings.definition: To raise a number to a given power; more broadly, to cause to grow at an exponential rate.
meanings.example: Network effects exponentiate the value of a platform as each additional user increases utility for all others.

meanings.definition: A system in which the output or effect of a process feeds back to affect the input or cause, either amplifying or dampening the original signal.
meanings.example: Climate models reveal dangerous positive feedback loops in which warming causes ice melt that leads to further warming.

meanings.definition: A proposed explanation for an observable phenomenon, formulated as a starting point for investigation.
meanings.example: The researcher formulated a hypothesis that a diet high in processed sugar impairs short-term memory.

meanings.definition: A system of mutually adapted parts working together; the means by which an effect is produced.
meanings.example: Scientists are still investigating the cellular mechanism by which the virus evades the immune system.

meanings.definition: A natural or established process by which something takes place.
meanings.example: The arbitration mechanism provides a structured alternative to litigation for resolving commercial disputes.

meanings.definition: Each of several possible ways in which a set of things can be ordered or arranged.
meanings.example: With five players and three positions, there are sixty permutations to consider when selecting the starting lineup.

meanings.definition: A complete transformation or alteration.
meanings.example: The latest permutation of the software included significant changes to the user interface.

meanings.definition: A person or thing that acts as a substitute for another; a measure used as an indicator for something else.
meanings.example: Researchers use satellite imagery as a proxy for economic activity in regions with limited data.

meanings.definition: The authority to represent someone, especially in voting.
meanings.example: She gave her proxy to a colleague, allowing him to vote on her behalf at the annual general meeting.

meanings.definition: The action of reproducing or making a copy of something; the reproduction of results in a scientific study by independent researchers.
meanings.example: The replication crisis in social psychology has prompted calls for more rigorous pre-registration of studies.

meanings.definition: In biology, the copying of a DNA molecule to produce two identical daughter strands.
meanings.example: Errors in DNA replication are the primary cause of mutations that can lead to cancer.

meanings.definition: Relating to a system as a whole rather than a part; affecting the entire body or organisation.
meanings.example: The investigation revealed systemic failings in the oversight of financial institutions, not isolated errors.

meanings.definition: Liability to change rapidly and unpredictably, especially for the worse; in finance, the rate of price fluctuation.
meanings.example: The high volatility of cryptocurrency markets deters many institutional investors from holding them.

meanings.definition: Tendency to evaporate rapidly; being volatile.
meanings.example: The volatility of the solvent made it essential to use it only in well-ventilated conditions.

meanings.definition: The act of washing oneself; a ceremonial act of washing in a religious context.
meanings.example: Before entering the mosque, worshippers performed their ritual ablutions at the outdoor fountains.

meanings.definition: To feel intense disgust or hatred for; to abhor something deeply.
meanings.example: She abominated the casual cruelty that passed for wit in some parts of the literary world.

meanings.definition: To make something holy or consecrate it; to give official approval or sanction to something.
meanings.example: The ritual was performed to sanctify the new building, invoking blessings on all who would work there.

meanings.definition: To cause someone to feel degraded or humiliated; to lower in rank, status, or respect.
meanings.example: He refused to abase himself before the committee, maintaining his dignity despite the hostile questioning.

meanings.definition: An official agreement or treaty; a formal settlement reached through negotiation.
meanings.example: The peace accord ended three decades of civil conflict and established a transitional justice mechanism.

meanings.definition: Agreement or harmony; correspondence of one thing with another.
meanings.example: Her actions were in full accord with the organisation's stated values and mission.

meanings.definition: A charge or claim that someone has done something wrong or illegal; the act of attributing blame.
meanings.example: The accusation of corruption was never substantiated and was later withdrawn with an apology.

meanings.definition: Giving sufficient reason for legal action.
meanings.example: The lawyer advised that the defamatory statements were actionable in court.

meanings.definition: Able to be acted upon; providing a clear basis for action.
meanings.example: The report provided actionable recommendations that the board implemented within six months.

meanings.definition: To formally determine or pronounce in a judicial setting; to rule or deem officially.
meanings.example: The court adjudged the defendant guilty on two of the four counts, acquitting on the remainder.

meanings.definition: Having the effect of making something better or less severe; tending to ameliorate.
meanings.example: The committee proposed a series of ameliorative measures to reduce the impact of the policy on low-income households.

meanings.definition: To use analogy in explaining something; to draw comparisons to illustrate a point.
meanings.example: He analogised the brain's activity to a symphony orchestra, each section playing its part within a larger composition.

meanings.definition: The process by which a person or group adopts the culture and identity of a new society.
meanings.example: The policy of forced assimilation had caused generations of indigenous communities to lose their languages.

meanings.definition: The absorption and integration of information or ideas.
meanings.example: The rapid assimilation of new technologies transformed the way businesses operated within a single decade.

meanings.definition: To make a stupid or careless mistake; to act clumsily or without judgement.
meanings.example: The government had badly blundered in its handling of the crisis, losing public confidence within weeks.

meanings.definition: To move clumsily or carelessly.
meanings.example: In the darkness, he blundered into the furniture and knocked over a lamp.

meanings.definition: A form of government or political organisation; a society that is organised as a state or other political entity.
meanings.example: The philosopher argued that a just polity could only be built on the equal participation of all its citizens.

meanings.definition: Evidence or proof of honesty and good intentions; credentials demonstrating good faith.
meanings.example: The delegation was asked to present its bona fides before negotiations could begin.

meanings.definition: The material evidence that a crime has been committed; the body of facts that constitute a breach of law.
meanings.example: The prosecution struggled to establish the corpus delicti without physical evidence linking the defendant to the scene.

meanings.definition: Enacted or enacted after the fact; retroactive.
meanings.example: The constitutional prohibition on ex post facto laws prevents the legislature from criminalising conduct that was legal when it occurred.

meanings.definition: Unforeseeable circumstances that prevent a party from fulfilling a contract; an act of God or other uncontrollable event.
meanings.example: The contract included a force majeure clause that suspended obligations in the event of a natural disaster.

meanings.definition: While not present; in the absence of a person, especially at a trial or ceremony.
meanings.example: The fugitive was convicted in absentia after fleeing the jurisdiction before the verdict was delivered.

meanings.definition: In private; said of legal proceedings conducted in secret, excluding the public and press.
meanings.example: The hearing was held in camera due to the sensitive nature of the evidence involving national security.

meanings.definition: In the place of a parent; acting with parental authority over someone who is not one's child.
meanings.example: When schools act in loco parentis, they take on a duty of care that extends to the physical and psychological wellbeing of their pupils.

meanings.definition: In position; in the original place; without moving from the usual location.
meanings.example: The archaeologists decided to study the artefacts in situ before carefully removing them from the site.

meanings.definition: As a whole; entirely; in its totality.
meanings.example: The committee rejected the proposal in toto, finding no merit in any of its constituent parts.

meanings.definition: Between living people; relating to a gift or transfer made during the lifetime of the donor.
meanings.example: An inter vivos trust allows the donor to transfer assets while still alive, avoiding probate.

meanings.definition: Making the necessary alterations while not affecting the main point; with the appropriate changes made.
meanings.example: The regulations applicable to banks apply mutatis mutandis to insurance companies.

meanings.definition: An incidental remark in a legal judgment, not essential to the decision and therefore not binding; a passing comment.
meanings.example: The judge's observation on the burden of proof was an obiter dictum and not binding on lower courts.

meanings.definition: At an equal pace or rate; with equal treatment, especially of creditors in insolvency.
meanings.example: Under the agreement, all creditors would be treated pari passu, receiving equal percentage recoveries on their claims.

meanings.definition: By or in itself; essentially; intrinsically.
meanings.example: Possession of the item is not per se an offence, but becomes one if the holder intends to use it unlawfully.

meanings.definition: A person who is unacceptable or unwelcome, especially a foreign diplomat officially declared unwelcome.
meanings.example: Following the espionage allegations, the ambassador was declared persona non grata and given forty-eight hours to leave.

meanings.definition: Proportionately; in proportion to a specified factor.
meanings.example: The dividend was paid pro rata to all shareholders based on the number of shares held.

meanings.definition: Seemingly; apparently; partly; resembling but not fully being.
meanings.example: The organisation had a quasi-governmental status, funded by the state but managed independently.

meanings.definition: A principle in international law that allows a treaty to be set aside if conditions change fundamentally from those at the time of signing.
meanings.example: The government invoked rebus sic stantibus to argue that the environmental situation had changed so fundamentally that the treaty obligations no longer applied.

meanings.definition: A legal doctrine meaning "the thing speaks for itself," allowing negligence to be inferred from the nature of an accident.
meanings.example: The plaintiff relied on res ipsa loquitur after the surgical instrument was found inside her body post-operation.

meanings.definition: With the main terms in the preceding statement the other way around.
meanings.example: The father's values were transmitted to the son, but the reverse was also true: the son influenced his father, and vice versa.

meanings.definition: In relation to; compared with; face to face with.
meanings.example: The report examined the company's competitive position vis-à-vis its main rivals.

meanings.definition: The voice of the people; popular opinion or sentiment.
meanings.example: The politician claimed to speak for the vox populi, though polls suggested otherwise.

meanings.definition: A standard or point of reference against which things can be measured or assessed.
meanings.example: The company set the FTSE 100 as its benchmark for evaluating portfolio performance.

meanings.definition: A marker surveyed to measure land elevations; a standardised test used to compare system performance.
meanings.example: Independent benchmark tests showed that the new chip outperformed its predecessor by thirty per cent.

meanings.definition: Secured by collateral; backed by assets pledged to guarantee a loan or obligation.
meanings.example: The bank refused to extend further credit unless the loan was fully collateralised against real property.

meanings.definition: The other party in a financial transaction or legal contract.
meanings.example: The bank's exposure depended not only on market prices but also on the creditworthiness of each counterparty.

meanings.definition: The quality of being fair and impartial; justice according to natural law as distinct from enacted law.
meanings.example: The judge invoked principles of equity to prevent an outcome that was technically legal but manifestly unjust.

meanings.definition: The value of a company's shares; ownership interest in a business.
meanings.example: Early employees were rewarded with equity in the company, which proved valuable at the IPO.

meanings.definition: Interchangeable; able to be replaced by an identical item; mutually substitutable.
meanings.example: Oil is a fungible commodity; one barrel is identical to another and traded as such.

meanings.definition: To limit one's risk by making balancing investments or commitments.
meanings.example: The importer hedged its currency exposure by buying forward contracts against a weakening domestic currency.

meanings.definition: To avoid committing oneself firmly; to qualify one's statements.
meanings.example: Politicians often hedge their answers on controversial topics to avoid alienating any segment of the electorate.

meanings.definition: The state of not having enough liquid assets to meet short-term obligations; difficulty in selling an asset quickly.
meanings.example: The bank's illiquidity became apparent when it could not meet withdrawal demands during the run.

meanings.definition: The availability of liquid assets; the ease with which an asset can be converted to cash; cash flow readiness.
meanings.example: Central banks inject liquidity into the financial system during crises to prevent credit markets from seizing up.

meanings.definition: The exclusive possession or control of the supply of a commodity or service.
meanings.example: The regulator broke up the telecommunications monopoly, allowing new entrants to compete for consumers.

meanings.definition: Exclusive possession, control, or exercise of something.
meanings.example: No single researcher has a monopoly on the truth; all contributions to the debate deserve scrutiny.

meanings.definition: The process of pooling assets and converting them into marketable securities; the packaging of financial instruments.
meanings.example: The securitisation of mortgage loans was central to the 2008 financial crisis, obscuring the underlying risk from investors.

meanings.definition: A sum of money granted by the government or a public body to help an industry or business keep the price of a commodity low.
meanings.example: Agricultural subsidies in wealthy nations were blamed for undermining farmers in developing countries.

meanings.definition: A tax or duty to be paid on a particular class of imports or exports.
meanings.example: The government imposed tariffs on steel imports to protect domestic manufacturers from foreign competition.

meanings.definition: A list of charges for services or prices for goods.
meanings.example: The hotel's room tariff was clearly posted at the front desk.

meanings.definition: The income return on an investment; the interest or dividend produced by a financial investment.
meanings.example: As bond yields rose, investors moved from equities to fixed income.

meanings.definition: The amount of a product or crop produced; output.
meanings.example: The drought reduced grain yield by nearly forty per cent in the affected regions.

meanings.definition: A mixture or blend of different elements; an alloy of mercury with another metal.
meanings.example: His philosophy was an amalgam of classical liberalism and contemporary environmentalism.

meanings.definition: The action of attributing something to a cause, person, or source.
meanings.example: The art historian's attribution of the painting to Rembrandt was disputed by colleagues who believed it to be by a pupil.

meanings.definition: In psychology, the process by which people explain the causes of behaviour.
meanings.example: Fundamental attribution error leads us to overemphasise personal character while underestimating situational factors.

meanings.definition: To relapse into bad habits, moral failure, or a previous negative state after improvement.
meanings.example: Despite years of sobriety, the pressure of the crisis caused him to backslide into old patterns.

meanings.definition: Insincere, pious, or moralistic talk; hypocritical language used for effect.
meanings.example: The politician's cant about family values was exposed when his own conduct was revealed to contradict them.

meanings.definition: The special vocabulary of a particular group, especially thieves; jargon.
meanings.example: The novel captured the cant of Victorian street traders with authentic detail.

meanings.definition: The relationship between cause and effect; the principle that every event has a preceding cause.
meanings.example: Establishing causality, rather than mere correlation, is the central challenge of epidemiological research.

meanings.definition: The supposed faculty of perceiving things beyond the normal range of the senses; extrasensory perception.
meanings.example: She claimed to possess clairvoyance, but scientists found no evidence of perceptions beyond the normal range.

meanings.definition: The ability to see what will happen in the future; unusually insightful foresight.
meanings.example: His clairvoyance in predicting market trends had earned him a legendary reputation among investors.

meanings.definition: The action of thinking deeply about something; earnest contemplation.
